Ayah 1–4 · linked video: 01. Al-Haj (Ayah 1-4)

Surah Al-Hajj: Commentary on Ayahs 1-4

Ayah 1

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ زَلْزَلَةَ ٱلسَّاعَةِ شَىْءٌ عَظِيمٌ

Surah Al-Hajj picks up on a subject that was left off in the previous Surah, Surah Al-Anbiya: the Great Disturbance, the Day of Judgment and its beginnings. This Surah is unique because it contains elements of both Makki and Madani revelation. Certain ayahs were clearly revealed after the Prophet ﷺ migrated to Medina. Some scholars held the opinion that the entire Surah is Madani, but the majority position holds that parts of the Surah are Makki, with small chunks revealed during the Prophet’s ﷺ migration itself. This is reported from Abdullah bin Abbas (RA).

This makes sense when we examine the Surah’s content. The ayahs that appear to be Madani either respond to and defend the Muslims or discuss Hajj. Of course, Hajj couldn’t be discussed in Makkah, but Allah ﷻ begins speaking about it the moment the Muslims leave Makkah.

The opening address provides another clear indication that much of this Surah is Makki. The ayah begins with يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ (O humanity), which is the dominant usage in Makkan Quran. In Madani Quran, we typically find يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (O you who believe), where Allah ﷻ addresses believers specifically. When a Surah begins with “O humanity,” it’s a strong indication of Makkan origin. There are some exceptions to this pattern, like the beginning of Surah An-Nisa, but there we find an immediate transition to addressing believers directly.

Allah ﷻ commands: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ - “O people, be conscious of your Master.” Then He declares: إِنَّ زَلْزَلَةَ ٱلسَّاعَةِ شَىْءٌ عَظِيمٌ - “Indeed, the shaking, the earthquake, the violent earthquake of the Hour is something tremendous.”

The word زَلْزَلَة in Arabic comes from زَلَّ which means to slip. When you take it to its intensified form, zalzalah conveys continuous slipping and faulting. The earth keeps shaking, and the people standing on it keep slipping—they cannot stand firm. When this shaking doesn’t stop, zalzalah is the perfect word to capture that reality. The Surah that describes this most vividly is one many of you have memorized: إِذَا زُلْزِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ زِلْزَالَهَا (When the earth is shaken with its earthquake). The verb is used twice—once as a verb and once as an affirmation of the verb—which means it will keep happening. It won’t stop. It will feel like an endless earthquake. Usually when earthquakes happen, how long do they last? A few seconds. All the damage is done in just a few seconds. But this shaking is going to continuously go on.

The phrase شَىْءٌ عَظِيمٌ (something tremendous, something huge) is significant because in the previous Surah, we find people treating these matters casually, even as entertainment. They crack jokes about the Day of Judgment and make smart comments about it. Now Allah ﷻ emphasizes: this is not something small. This is a big deal.

Ayah 2

يَوْمَ تَرَوْنَهَا تَذْهَلُ كُلُّ مُرْضِعَةٍ عَمَّآ أَرْضَعَتْ وَتَضَعُ كُلُّ ذَاتِ حَمْلٍ حَمْلَهَا وَتَرَى ٱلنَّاسَ سُكَـٰرَىٰ وَمَا هُم بِسُكَـٰرَىٰ وَلَـٰكِنَّ عَذَابَ ٱللَّهِ شَدِيدٌ

We’re speaking here about a time when all the believers and their souls have been taken away. Allah ﷻ describes what happens on the day when people witness this earthquake: يَوْمَ تَرَوْنَهَا تَذْهَلُ كُلُّ مُرْضِعَةٍ عَمَّآ أَرْضَعَتْ - “On the day you see it, every nursing mother will forget what she was nursing.”

A مُرْضِعَة is a mother who is feeding a baby—whether human or animal. As long as the mother is feeding from her own body, she’s called a murdi’ah. The ayah doesn’t say she forgets her child or her offspring. It says عَمَّآ أَرْضَعَتْ - “whatever she used to feed”—as though she can’t even remember its name or what it is. That’s how shocking the Day of Judgment will be.

The word تَذْهَلُ captures forgetting something out of shock, forgetting something out of terror, being stunned as a result of fear. She’s feeding the child—imagine how protective a mother is of her child. The safest place described in Arabic expression is مَهْد, meaning the cradle. This word is even used sarcastically in the Quran: فَبِئْسَ الْمِهَادُ (What a horrible cradle that will be). Just as a mother hugs her baby, Hellfire will hug and squeeze those who enter it.

But here, Allah ﷻ highlights that she will drop the child. Every feeding mother will forget. This is significant because the time when a mother is most directly connected with her child, and the child is most directly connected to and dependent on the mother, is during nursing. There’s nothing quite like that time. It’s a special bond between mother and child. During this period, the mother forgets that her husband exists, that other children exist—she has to feed the baby and take care of this one child above everyone else. Yet Allah ﷻ describes her forgetting عَمَّآ أَرْضَعَتْ—whatever it was that she fed, as though she doesn’t even remember, recall or care what it was.

وَتَضَعُ كُلُّ ذَاتِ حَمْلٍ حَمْلَهَا - “And every pregnant one will drop her load.” There will be miscarriages. The continuous violent earthquakes will lead to every single pregnant creature on the earth having a miscarriage.

وَتَرَى ٱلنَّاسَ سُكَـٰرَىٰ - “And you will see people looking drunk.” The use of تَرَى (you will see) is powerful—Allah ﷻ wants us to visualize what’s going to happen. You’re going to see people walking around drunk. When someone is drunk, they’re tipsy, they can’t stand straight, they’re not balanced. The earth is shaking so much that people will appear this way. If you’ve ever boarded a ship when it’s wavy, or you’re on a plane during turbulence walking from the restroom, you know what people look like in that state.

سُكَـٰرَىٰ describes the look of drunkenness. This word can refer to being drunk from alcohol, but it’s also used figuratively. You might say someone is “love drunk”—سَكْرَانُ بِالْحُبِّ—they can’t even stand straight, they’re always in this daze. There’s also a religious phrase, سَكْرَةُ الْمَوْتِ (the drunkenness of death), describing the look when death arrives—your eyes rolling over, going in and out of consciousness.

وَمَا هُم بِسُكَـٰرَىٰ - “But they are not actually drunk,” meaning not drunk from alcohol at all. وَلَـٰكِنَّ عَذَابَ ٱللَّهِ شَدِيدٌ - “However, the punishment of Allah ﷻ is intense.” This is what causes their state—not intoxication, but the overwhelming reality of Allah’s ﷻ punishment.

Ayah 3

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يُجَـٰدِلُ فِى ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَيَتَّبِعُ كُلَّ شَيْطَـٰنٍ مَّرِيدٍ

Despite all of this—despite this heavy reality that’s coming, this horrific state—Allah ﷻ tells us: وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ (from among the people). The phrase مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ literally means “from the people,” which actually indicates a certain category of people.

There’s a certain category of people مَن يُجَـٰدِلُ فِى ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ - who like to debate and argue, going back and forth on the subject of Allah ﷻ as though it’s a casual subject, without any knowledge. وَيَتَّبِعُ كُلَّ شَيْطَـٰنٍ مَّرِيدٍ - “And this kind of person ends up following every devil that is ahead of all else in his rebellion.”

مَرِيد means to get ahead of the line in rebellion. When people are messed up and someone decides to do something really messed up—like when there’s a riot and people are making noise, and someone goes further and says, “Let me go burn a car”—that person goes out of everybody else’s way in wrongdoing. That’s what shaytaan marid describes.

This conversation is especially important in our times. We’re living in intellectually free times. Young people go to college and are exposed to all kinds of ideas. We’re in the age of the Internet, social media and YouTube, where good and bad ideas are all mixed together. You’re exposed to all kinds of things all the time. When you get exposed to these ideas, they stay in the back of your mind. Sometimes people develop questions about faith. They start questioning Allah ﷻ, questioning the existence of God, questioning the validity of Islam. These kinds of questions can wander inside the mind of a young person.

There are two realities inside of us, and these two realities are best understood through what’s alluded to in this Surah. There’s our physical self and our non-physical self—our unseen self. We have a physical heart, physical eyes, physical ears, all these physical faculties, and we have a physical mind, a brain that does all the thinking. But then we have an unseen heart. We have two kinds of hearts: the seen heart that you can do surgery on, the one that can be replaced, the one that can be recharged with a defibrillator—that’s the physical heart. These eyes with the retina that you put lenses or glasses on—those are your physical eyes.

But then Allah ﷻ talks about the metaphysical heart, the non-physical heart. That’s the heart that can get hard, harder than stone. That’s not the heart literally inside you that you can see in an X-ray. That’s a different heart—an unseen reality of the human being. There’s a seen dimension of the human being and an unseen dimension.

Think of it like a person inside a giant robot, controlling the robot from the inside. There’s the robot and there’s a person inside. Similarly, there’s an invisible reality inside you and me. One of my teachers explained it by asking, “Where are you?” When I pointed at myself, he said, “No, that’s your chest.” When I pointed at my head, he said, “No, that’s your brain—just gray matter, mushy stuff. Where are you? Where is Nouman?” He helped me understand: this body is just an outfit I’m wearing, and this outfit is going to expire. I will remain. This body itself is a kind of clothing, a garment we’re wearing, and it’s going to come off one day. We’re going to leave this body. We take this garment off every night when we go to sleep, and we come back into it. This is an outfit that Allah ﷻ has given us.

On the seen side, you have your mind, your brain, the neurons firing, your logical self. On the unseen side, you have your heart, your spiritual self. What happens when you get exposed to corrupt ideas is your spiritual self starts getting weaker while you feel like your intellectual self is getting stronger. In Islam, these two are supposed to be in harmony with each other. They’re supposed to remain healthy together. We don’t just feed our heart—we feed our mind too. But we don’t just feed our mind and neglect our heart, because that can kill our heart.

A person can reach the point where they start talking about Allah ﷻ fearlessly. They say things about Allah ﷻ even in the form of questions. For example, someone once emailed me asking, “How can Allah ﷻ punish if He calls Himself merciful? How come He punishes?” I responded from the Quran’s point of view, and she replied, “You gave a faith-based answer, but I want to know how Allah ﷻ can call Himself merciful from a logical point of view. I need an intellectual response. I don’t need a spiritual response.”

What that tells you is: God needs to meet my philosophical expectations. He needs to meet my logical expectations. God has to come to me—I don’t have to go to Him. You know what that reveals? The heart is dead. The heart is gone. All that’s left is a mind that’s ready to argue.

When all that’s left is the mind, there’s a problem: the place where humility rests is not the mind. Where does humility rest? In the heart. So when your humility is gone, the arguments of the Quran become wasted, set aside. They don’t mean anything to you anymore.

This is the kind of person who can debate about Allah ﷻ. And when they debate about Him, they follow the worst shaytaan—the one who goes out of his way to say the most obscene things about Allah ﷻ. They quote those opinions and call those kinds of things knowledge. That’s when the inside, the unseen of yourself, is being killed, and all that remains is the seen.

Ayah 4

كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ أَنَّهُۥ مَن تَوَلَّاهُ فَأَنَّهُۥ يُضِلُّهُۥ وَيَهْدِيهِ إِلَىٰ عَذَابِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ

كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ - “It has been mandated on him.” The word shaytaan comes from one of two possible origins: one who has lost hope entirely, or one who is filled with rage. Where does hope rest? In the heart. So the only thing left in shaytaan’s heart is anger.

People go through different psychological states. There’s a state in which they experience sadness, and that sadness over time, if not dealt with, turns into rage. For example, when a child is picked on at school or by siblings, the child is sad. They tell their mom and nobody listens. What does that neglect turn into? Anger. The next time they’re picked on, they lash out, because sadness has transformed into anger. That’s حُزْن (sadness) leading into غَضَب (anger)—that’s shaytaan. This person has only two emotional states left: either sadness or anger, because the alleviation from sadness comes from belief in Allah ﷻ. Allah ﷻ is the reason we can get rid of our sadness. We’ll learn about this at the end of the Surah in a powerful way.

These are the people following shaytaan. But what about the other people who follow Allah ﷻ? What is their internal state like? What are their emotional states? This is a deeply psychological Surah in that sense, both at the beginning and the end.

مَن تَوَلَّاهُ - “Whoever befriends him”—whoever befriends shaytaan—فَأَنَّهُۥ يُضِلُّهُۥ - “then he is going to mislead him.” وَيَهْدِيهِ إِلَىٰ عَذَابِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ - “And he’s going to guide him.” The only thing shaytaan can guide a person to is the punishment of a torturing flame.

Ayah 5–10 · linked video: 02. Al-Haj (Ayah 5-10)

Surah Al-Hajj: Ayahs 5-10

Ayah 5

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِن كُنتُمْ فِى رَيْبٍ مِّنَ ٱلْبَعْثِ فَإِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰكُم مِّن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ عَلَقَةٍ ثُمَّ مِن مُّضْغَةٍ مُّخَلَّقَةٍ وَغَيْرِ مُخَلَّقَةٍ لِّنُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ ۚ وَنُقِرُّ فِى ٱلْأَرْحَامِ مَا نَشَآءُ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ثُمَّ نُخْرِجُكُمْ طِفْلًا ثُمَّ لِتَبْلُغُوٓا۟ أَشُدَّكُمْ ۖ وَمِنكُم مَّن يُتَوَفَّىٰ وَمِنكُم مَّن يُرَدُّ إِلَىٰٓ أَرْذَلِ ٱلْعُمُرِ لِكَيْلَا يَعْلَمَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ عِلْمٍ شَيْـًٔا ۚ وَتَرَى ٱلْأَرْضَ هَامِدَةً فَإِذَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهَا ٱلْمَآءَ ٱهْتَزَّتْ وَرَبَتْ وَأَنۢبَتَتْ مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍۭ بَهِيجٍ

Allah ﷻ addresses all of humanity with a powerful argument about resurrection. If you have any doubt about being raised again after death, then consider how We created you from dust, then from a fluid (the sperm), then from a hanging piece of flesh.

The word عَلَقَةٍ literally means something that clings or hangs. The Arabs use this root to describe something that gets stuck, like when an idea gets stuck in your mind. This describes the early embryo that clings to the wall of the mother’s womb, suspended inside her belly. When people see ultrasound images today, they witness exactly what Allah ﷻ described centuries ago—that tiny, hanging piece of flesh visible on the screen.

Then Allah ﷻ describes it as مُّضْغَةٍ مُّخَلَّقَةٍ وَغَيْرِ مُخَلَّقَةٍ—a lump of flesh, formed and unformed. The word mukhalaqah refers to an early stage of sculpting. Think about when an artist doesn’t want you to see their work in progress because you wouldn’t recognize what it will become. When you first see an ultrasound and observe that little form, you might think, “That’s a baby? Really?” That’s the mukhalaqah stage—barely any signs that this will become a human being.

Then comes the ghayri mukhalaqah stage, when the formation becomes more apparent. Now you can distinguish what will be the head, the arms, the legs. The gender becomes visible. The human form begins to emerge. These are stages of development that the Arabs had no way of observing, yet Allah ﷻ described them with remarkable precision. Modern medical science has produced extensive literature on the Quran’s accurate description of embryonic development. Professor Mustafa Ahmed, who headed the Foundation for Islamic Education in Villanova, Pennsylvania, wrote particularly compelling research on this subject, comparing the Quranic description of fetal development with modern medical science.

Allah ﷻ tells us He describes these stages لِّنُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ—so We can clarify to all of you. Then He says, وَنُقِرُّ فِى ٱلْأَرْحَامِ مَا نَشَآءُ—and We maintain inside the wombs whatever We want. The word قَرَّ means to find a place and stay there, to be stationed. Allah ﷻ maintains and stations in the wombs whatever He wills. Sometimes the baby stays, sometimes it’s lost. What qualities this child will have—functioning limbs or defects, complexion, gender, intelligence—all of it belongs to His decree.

This continues إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى—until a well-defined, appointed time. Then ثُمَّ نُخْرِجُكُمْ طِفْلًا—We bring you out as a baby. When you hear this ayah, you should think about yourself. Allah ﷻ is saying, “I delivered you. We brought you out as a baby.”

Then He says, ثُمَّ لِتَبْلُغُوٓا۟ أَشُدَّكُمْ—then so you can reach your mature age, your physical strength, your adulthood. But not everyone follows the same path. وَمِنكُم مَّن يُتَوَفَّىٰ—among you are those who are taken away from this life, those who die.

وَمِنكُم مَّن يُرَدُّ إِلَىٰٓ أَرْذَلِ ٱلْعُمُرِ—and among you are those who are sent back to the most feeble state of age. The word يُرَدُّ means “sent back,” like when something is returned. Every night we experience a form of death in our sleep, and every morning Allah ﷻ sends us back to life. That’s why we say the morning dua about dying and being brought back to life. It’s as though every night Allah ﷻ receives us in a state like a coma, and He decides: “No, send them back. Let them live another day.”

But some people, Allah ﷻ keeps sending back day after day until they reach أَرْذَلِ ٱلْعُمُر—that deteriorated state of extreme old age. This is when people become physically disabled, unable to care for themselves, when bodily functions fail and dignity becomes difficult to maintain. In modern Western society, this often leads to seniors being placed in care facilities, isolated from family. People become uncomfortable around them, even disgusted by the realities of advanced age.

At this stage, people often treat the elderly as if they have nothing left to offer. “What good are you now? You can’t contribute anything. Society has to take care of you.” Memory loss sets in, conversations are forgotten moments after they happen. Yet in our tradition, we’re commanded to honor our elders and see them as a reminder—a warning of what awaits us all.

The Prophet ﷺ used to make dua seeking Allah’s protection from reaching such an advanced age where one becomes unable to care for oneself. It’s an extremely difficult state. One of its most painful aspects is the complete role reversal: you spent your life taking care of your children, doing everything for them without complaint. Now you’re completely dependent on those same children, but unlike how you served them, you can sense their discomfort, see the burden on their faces even when they don’t say it. This awareness eats away at you inside.

Our parents know our emotions before we can even show them. A mother can tell when her baby is sad before the baby can express it. Similarly, elderly parents sense their children’s resentment and impatience. They can’t walk away—they don’t have the physical ability. They can’t drive off—they don’t even have a license anymore.

Allah ﷻ describes this stage: لِكَيْلَا يَعْلَمَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ عِلْمٍ شَيْـًٔا—so that he doesn’t know anything after having known. Memory fades, knowledge disappears, and what comes after this state? Death.

Then Allah ﷻ transitions to a parallel image: وَتَرَى ٱلْأَرْضَ هَامِدَةً—and you see the earth dry and barren. Notice the remarkable transition in this ayah. We began with a baby in the womb, then became adults, then elderly and withered, our knowledge fading. The only thing left after this is death, and when a person dies, where do they go? Into the earth, that dry and lifeless land.

فَإِذَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهَا ٱلْمَآءَ ٱهْتَزَّتْ—then when We send water onto it, the earth shakes and vibrates. With every drop of water, it vibrates. وَرَبَتْ —and it rises. As the roots sprout and plants emerge, the earth itself appears to rise, as if it grew higher with the plants.

وَأَنۢبَتَتْ مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍۭ بَهِيجٍ—and it sprouts every kind of beautiful pair. The word بَهِيج describes stunning beauty—the earth becomes beautiful with greenery, flowers, and diverse textures.

But notice: this ayah didn’t start by talking about plants. It began with human beings. Allah ﷻ is drawing a parallel. Just as plants are raised from the dead earth, you will be raised from the ground. Those who believed won’t be alone—they’ll be in beautiful pairs, just like the paired plants. Every time you witness rain bringing life to dead earth, it’s a reminder that Allah ﷻ will bring you out of the ground, along with your parents, grandparents, and all their ancestors before them.

Ayah 6

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَأَنَّهُۥ يُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَأَنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

ذَٰلِكَ—all of that which we just described—بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ—is because Allah ﷻ Himself is the ultimate reality, the absolute truth. Everything we witness in creation points back to this fundamental reality.

وَأَنَّهُۥ يُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ—and He, in fact, is the One Who gives life to the dead. Haven’t you seen the plants emerge from dead soil? Allah ﷻ made that happen. One of the reasons He gives you this observable phenomenon—plants constantly emerging from the ground—is so you remember that resurrection is coming.

وَأَنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ—and He is in complete control over every single thing. Nothing lies outside His power, including bringing the dead back to life.

Ayah 7

وَأَنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ ءَاتِيَةٌ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهَا وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَبْعَثُ مَن فِى ٱلْقُبُورِ

وَأَنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ ءَاتِيَةٌ—and that the Hour is coming. Allah ﷻ is tying the entire argument together. The word أَنَّ at the beginning connects this statement to everything that came before. It’s a grammatical connector showing this conclusion flows directly from the evidence presented.

لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهَا—there is absolutely no doubt whatsoever in it. The certainty is absolute.

وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَبْعَثُ مَن فِى ٱلْقُبُورِ—and if it hasn’t been spelled out clearly enough, let Me make it explicit: Allah ﷻ is the One Who will raise whoever has been in the graves. He will pull them out.

Consider the parallel: In the beginning of this passage, Allah ﷻ described how He took you out of your mother’s belly—a place where you were resting, your entire world—and pulled you out into a new existence. The next time, it’s the earth holding you. The first time your mother was pregnant with you; the second time the earth is pregnant with you. And you will be delivered again.

Ayah 8

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يُجَـٰدِلُ فِى ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَلَا هُدًى وَلَا كِتَـٰبٍ مُّنِيرٍ

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يُجَـٰدِلُ فِى ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ—and still, there are some people who argue about Allah ﷻ without any knowledge. Allah ﷻ already mentioned this type of person earlier in the surah. When someone repeats themselves, especially in a tone of correction, it’s a form of scolding. It’s like a teacher addressing the class: “And there are STILL some people who don’t do their homework!” Everyone knows who that’s directed at.

Every time Allah ﷻ repeats this critique, it’s meant to dig deeper into your heart. It should make you reflect: Am I one of these people who argues without knowledge?

وَلَا هُدًى—and they don’t base their arguments on any kind of guidance. وَلَا كِتَـٰبٍ مُّنِيرٍ—nor on a book that gives off light. Notice the specific description: not just a book full of light (munawwar), but munir—a book that actively gives off light, illuminating everything around it. This is Allah ﷻ referring to the Quran.

The Quran possesses three qualities mentioned here: it contains knowledge, it provides guidance, and it’s a book that radiates light—meaning the book itself is blessed, and everything in its environment benefits from it. This echoes what Allah ﷻ said in the previous surah about the Quran being full of barakah (blessing).

Ayah 9

ثَانِىَ عِطْفِهِۦ لِيُضِلَّ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ لَهُۥ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا خِزْىٌ ۖ وَنُذِيقُهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ

ثَانِىَ عِطْفِهِۦ—twisting his neck, turning his shoulder. The word عِطْف refers to the neck or shoulders. In Arabic, when someone walks away turning their shoulder, it’s an expression showing pride and scorn. This person hears the message of the Quran and literally turns away, showing disdain.

Allah ﷻ captures this moment like a security camera catching a crime in progress. Imagine someone listening to the Quran, and the moment they hear it, they turn their shoulders and walk away as if to say, “I don’t need this,” and they leave. That gesture itself is captured as a crime in the Quran.

This isn’t just walking away—it’s walking away with arrogance, showing pride. It suggests the person has some status that makes them feel entitled to dismiss divine revelation. No one without social standing acts arrogant, because who would they display it to? But a person with influence over others, someone people recognize for their knowledge, status, or political position—when such a person turns away, it sends a message.

لِيُضِلَّ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ—so that he may misguide others from the path of Allah ﷻ. Notice it doesn’t say لِيَضِلَّ—“so he may be misguided.” It says يُضِلَّ—“so he may misguide OTHERS.” Just by turning away, this person influences others to turn away as well. People observe and think, “He’s not impressed, so I shouldn’t be impressed either.”

We learn here that people of influence face a particular test. It’s extremely difficult for influential people not to become arrogant. When they give in to that temptation—all that attention, all those followers—it affects them. And when they turn away from truth, they’re held responsible not just for their own actions, but for everyone they influence.

These are celebrities, people with millions of followers. Their actions get replicated by masses—millions, thousands, dozens, even just a handful. Regardless of the number, as long as you have any level of influence, you’re answerable for how you use it.

Consider modern teen celebrities and pop stars—the immense influence they wield over young people. When such a person does something shameful or indecent, the effects are colossal. Young Muslim girls walk to Islamic school with backpacks featuring these celebrities’ images. They want to dress like them, talk like them, act like them. Parents struggle: “Why do you want to dress like that? Why can’t you wear something modest? Why the tight clothes?” Where does this come from? From those celebrities on TV. That influence trickles down through the entire society.

لَهُۥ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا خِزْىٌ—for such a person, there is humiliation in this world. If they’re exposed to revelation and turn away from it, they will face public humiliation. خِزْى means embarrassment shown in front of others, public disgrace.

وَنُذِيقُهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ—and We will make him taste, on the Day of Resurrection, the punishment of a fire that burns everything down. The word حَرِيق describes an all-consuming blaze.

Ayah 10

ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ يَدَاكَ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ

ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ يَدَاكَ—that is because of what your two hands have sent forward. The literal translation is “what your hands sent forth,” but the meaning is: these are the investments you made, this is what you were working toward. You thought you were building a bright future for yourself—well, this is that future you were working on.

This connects directly to the previous ayah’s mention of punishment. Allah ﷻ is saying: You chose this path. These are the consequences of the choices you made with your own hands.

وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ—and Allah ﷻ is not—not at all—someone who wrongs His slaves. The structure لَيْسَ بِ provides strong emphasis on the negation. Allah ﷻ doesn’t wrong anyone. Every person receives exactly what they earned through their own choices and actions. The punishment isn’t injustice—it’s the natural consequence of rejecting truth despite having been shown clear evidence.

Ayah 11–18 · linked video: 03. Al-Haj (Ayah 11-18)

Surah Al-Hajj: Ayahs 11-18

Ayah 11

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَعْبُدُ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ حَرْفٍ ۖ فَإِنْ أَصَابَهُۥ خَيْرٌ ٱطْمَأَنَّ بِهِۦ ۖ وَإِنْ أَصَابَتْهُ فِتْنَةٌ ٱنقَلَبَ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهِۦ خَسِرَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْخُسْرَانُ ٱلْمُبِينُ

Previously, Allah ﷻ described someone who completely rejects the religion and turns away in arrogance. Now, a different problematic situation emerges. There exists a certain category of people from humanity who worship Allah ﷻ, but they do so عَلَىٰ حَرْفٍ – on the edge, at the brink.

The word حَرْف literally means the edge of a cliff. Picture someone walking along the narrow ridge of a mountain, where a steep drop awaits on either side. When you walk on a harf, you’re forced to watch every step with extreme caution, uncertain whether to proceed forward or turn back. This captures the fragile nature of this person’s commitment to Islam. They’re Muslim, but barely holding on, constantly wavering.

This imagery parallels the language we find in Surah Al-Baqarah. Just as that surah presents parables of people walking in darkness when lightning strikes, Surah Al-Hajj presents the image of someone teetering on a cliff’s edge. Both surahs share remarkable similarities in their subject matter, both discussing Hajj, both speaking about Prophet Ibrahim (AS), both using powerful parables about people on uncertain ground.

The straight path that Allah ﷻ describes throughout the Quran is, by definition, a wide path where one can walk with confidence. But this person’s relationship with faith is anything but confident. فَإِنْ أَصَابَهُۥ خَيْرٌ ٱطْمَأَنَّ بِهِۦ – if good comes his way, if life is easy, then he becomes content. He’s happy with his faith when things are going well.

وَإِنْ أَصَابَتْهُ فِتْنَةٌ ٱنقَلَبَ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهِۦ – but if a trial strikes him, if difficulty enters his life, he flips entirely. Notice the language here: he doesn’t just turn his shoulder like the arrogant person mentioned before. He performs a complete turnaround, انقَلَبَ – he does a full 180-degree turn. The mention of his face is particularly significant because Islam is fundamentally about putting your face on the ground in prostration. His turning his face away represents the ultimate rejection, as the face is also the seat of pride in human expression.

You’ll recognize people like this. They don’t know much about Islam, they’re already living on the edge of faith. Then when something difficult happens in the family, when hardship strikes, the first question they ask is: “How come God did this to me?” They say, “I don’t need to pray anymore. I used to pray maybe once a year, but even that’s too much now.”

خَسِرَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ – this person has lost both this world and the next. ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْخُسْرَانُ ٱلْمُبِينُ – that is the ultimate, most clear and obvious form of loss. The ayah spells out exactly what he has lost: no peace, no goodness, no tranquility in this world, and no salvation in the hereafter.

Ayah 12

يَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَضُرُّهُۥ وَمَا لَا يَنفَعُهُۥ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلضَّلَـٰلُ ٱلْبَعِيدُ

This person begins calling upon things besides Allah ﷻ, entities that can neither harm nor benefit him. ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلضَّلَـٰلُ ٱلْبَعِيدُ – that is the ultimate misguidance, the furthest deviation from truth.

Consider the irony: these are often the same people who claim to have intellectual problems with faith. Yet these self-proclaimed rationalists are reading horoscopes, consulting fortune tellers, carrying lucky charms, wearing their “lucky color” to important events, taking their “lucky pen” to exams. They avoid going out on Friday the 13th, they worry when a black cat crosses their path. These supposedly sophisticated intellectuals who pride themselves on reason have no problem embracing the most baseless superstitions.

You find this even in the most “academic” regions. In New York City, the metropolis of skyscrapers, buildings skip from the 12th floor directly to the 14th. Whether you label it 14 or 13, it remains the 13th floor, but they refuse to acknowledge it as such. In the heart of Manhattan’s financial district, where real estate costs can exceed $20,000 monthly for a mere 500 square feet, you’ll find palm readers and psychic shops thriving on the ground floors of major office buildings. Why? Because before making multimillion-dollar deals, Harvard MBAs stop by to ask what color tie they should wear to their merger meeting.

This is the ultimate misguidance Allah ﷻ speaks of – abandoning the Creator for things that possess no power whatsoever.

Ayah 13

يَدْعُوا۟ لَمَن ضَرُّهُۥٓ أَقْرَبُ مِن نَّفْعِهِۦ ۚ لَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَوْلَىٰ وَلَبِئْسَ ٱلْعَشِيرُ

He calls upon that whose harm is closer to him than any benefit. When someone turns to false objects of worship, they’re calling upon something that will definitely harm them. The harm is inevitable because they’ve committed a grave offense against Allah ﷻ. Any hoped-for benefit is impossible, but the harm is guaranteed.

لَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَوْلَىٰ – what a horrible protective friend, what a terrible guardian he has chosen. This reveals one of the key motivations people have for choosing false gods: they seek protection. They want someone or something to shield them from life’s uncertainties. But what a miserable protector they’ve selected.

وَلَبِئْسَ ٱلْعَشِيرُ – and what a horrible family member, what a terrible companion. People often consider themselves spiritually connected to their chosen deities, sometimes even claiming to be “children” of those gods. The word عَشِيرُ refers to extended family, the people you interact with constantly, those you live with intimately. It’s used in the Quran when describing how spouses should treat each other: وَعَاشِرُوهُنَّ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ – “live with them in kindness.” The term implies ongoing, close interaction.

So Allah ﷻ is saying: what a terrible gang you’ve chosen, what a horrible crew you’ve joined. You’ve selected the worst possible family to align yourself with.

Ayah 14

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ مَا يُرِيدُ

After describing those who fall away and those who turn to false gods, Allah ﷻ now completes the picture with certainty: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ – indeed, Allah will cause to enter, He will admit those who believed and performed the righteous deeds expected of them into جَنَّـٰتٍ – gardens.

This brings us back to the profound gardening metaphor that began earlier in the surah. You started in the garden of your mother’s womb. Then you were brought out into this temporary garden of the world. Then you’re buried again like a seed in the earth. Then you’re raised up like a plant emerging from soil. And finally, you’re placed in the ultimate, eternal garden. The entire cycle of cultivation is now complete.

تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ – gardens beneath which rivers flow, with permanent irrigation sustaining them forever. إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ مَا يُرِيدُ – indeed, Allah does whatever He wills. This is the will of Allah ﷻ, and His will is always executed.

Ayah 15

مَن كَانَ يَظُنُّ أَن لَّن يَنصُرَهُ ٱللَّهُ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ فَلْيَمْدُدْ بِسَبَبٍ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ ثُمَّ لْيَقْطَعْ فَلْيَنظُرْ هَلْ يُذْهِبَنَّ كَيْدُهُۥ مَا يَغِيظُ

Whoever assumes, whoever has built the conviction that Allah will not help him in this world or in the next – this ayah addresses a specific psychological state that many people experience on their spiritual journey.

Consider someone actively working on their faith, trying to become more committed to Islam, striving to be a better Muslim. They’re putting in effort, but they don’t see results. They don’t perceive personal change in themselves. They say, “I’ve been reciting Quran but I don’t feel anything.” They’re involved in dawah work but programs aren’t succeeding, people aren’t responding, things aren’t working out. Eventually, they begin losing hope. They think, “This isn’t helping me. What’s the point of all this effort? I’m not getting anywhere. Allah isn’t going to help me in this world or the next.”

To such a person, Allah ﷻ says: فَلْيَمْدُدْ بِسَبَبٍ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ – then let him extend a rope to the sky. The word سَبَب means rope, specifically the kind of rope used to climb palm trees to pick dates – you throw it up and scale the tree. This imagery of a rope connecting earth to sky has already appeared in the Quran: اعْتَصِمُوا بِحَبْلِ اللَّهِ جَمِيعًا – “Hold fast to the rope of Allah, all together.” The Prophet ﷺ described this as “the outstretched rope from Allah, extending from the sky to the earth.” We hold one end while Allah ﷻ holds the other. The Quran we recite is connected directly to the preserved tablet with Allah ﷻ. When we hold onto our copy, we’re holding onto the rope that reaches all the way to Him.

ثُمَّ لْيَقْطَعْ – then let him cut it. This seems shocking at first. When you progress in religion, you rise in ranks. يَرْفَعِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجَاتٍ – “Allah raises those who believe among you and those given knowledge in degrees.” Spiritual advancement is like climbing upward. So Allah ﷻ is saying: You’ve made progress, you’ve climbed high – why would you cut the rope now?

If you cut the rope after climbing, isn’t that suicidal? The fall from a height is far worse than never having climbed at all. Someone who made significant progress in faith and then severed their connection will come crashing down with devastating force.

فَلْيَنظُرْ هَلْ يُذْهِبَنَّ كَيْدُهُۥ مَا يَغِيظُ – then let him see whether his plan removes what enrages him. The person’s frustration led them to think, “I’m done with this. I tried Islam seriously and it didn’t do anything for me. I’m cutting the rope.” But did cutting yourself off from Allah ﷻ actually eliminate your frustration? Are you at peace now? Are you feeling better?

The reality is the opposite. You’ve severed yourself from the one thing that could have given you genuine hope. You’ve been slammed back to earth, becoming purely materialistic. The very thing that could have removed the anger and turmoil from your heart – the Quran, your connection to Allah ﷻ – is now gone from your life. So tell me: did cutting the rope remove what was boiling your blood and making you angry?

Ayah 16

وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَهْدِى مَن يُرِيدُ

And thus – in this manner, with this clarity – We have sent it down as آيَاتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ, absolutely clear signs, miraculous revelations that are self-evident in their truth.

وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَهْدِى مَن يُرِيدُ – and indeed Allah guides whom He wills. This statement carries two dimensions. On one level, it affirms that guidance is entirely in Allah’s hands. On another level, يُرِيدُ can be understood as referring to the person who truly wants guidance. If you genuinely want to be guided, if you sincerely seek it and don’t cut the rope, Allah ﷻ will guide you.

Ayah 17

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ وَٱلصَّـٰبِـِٔينَ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ وَٱلْمَجُوسَ وَٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ

Allah ﷻ now enumerates different religious groups that exist in the world:

الَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ – those who believe, the Muslims.

الَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ – those who are Jewish.

الصَّـٰبِـِٔينَ – the Sabians. This group has been mentioned before in Surahs Al-Baqarah, Al-Imran and Al-Ma’idah. Some scholars identify them as Zoroastrians or fire worshippers, while others describe them as people who believed in Prophet Nuh (AS) but no subsequent prophets.

النَّصَـٰرَىٰ – the Christians.

الْمَجُوسَ – the Magians. The dominant scholarly position identifies these as the Zoroastrians from the Persian tradition, the followers of ancient Iranian religion.

الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوٓا۟ – those who commit shirk, who associate partners with Allah ﷻ. This category doesn’t only include the polytheists of Mecca. It encompasses all forms of polytheism: Buddhists, Hindus, and every manifestation of worship directed toward anything other than Allah ﷻ alone.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ – among all these groups, Allah will make the decisive judgment between them on the Day of Resurrection, on the Day of Standing. Every group claims to possess the truth. Every religion argues for its validity. But all arguments will be settled definitively on that Day.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ – indeed, Allah is over all things a constant Witness. He witnesses every claim, every action, every belief. Nothing escapes His observation. He will judge based on perfect, complete knowledge of everything.

Ayah 18

أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَسْجُدُ لَهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ وَٱلنُّجُومُ وَٱلْجِبَالُ وَٱلشَّجَرُ وَٱلدَّوَآبُّ وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۖ وَكَثِيرٌ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْعَذَابُ ۗ وَمَن يُهِنِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن مُّكْرِمٍ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَآءُ

أَلَمْ تَرَ – Do you not see? Haven’t you observed this reality?

أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَسْجُدُ لَهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ – that everything and everyone in the heavens and the earth prostrates to Allah. But wait – Hindus don’t prostrate to Allah ﷻ. Many Christians don’t make sujud. Jews don’t perform this act. How can the ayah claim universal prostration?

The answer lies in understanding the two dimensions of our existence: our physical self and our metaphysical, spiritual self. For believers, both our physical body and our spiritual heart make sujud to Allah ﷻ. But even for the disbeliever, his physical self continues in a state of prostration and submission to Allah ﷻ.

His heart beats by Allah’s command – each heartbeat is an act of worship and submission, whether he acknowledges it or not. Every cell in his body taking in oxygen and releasing it is worshiping Allah ﷻ, submitting to the laws Allah ﷻ has set in motion. His lungs expand and contract in obedience to Allah ﷻ. Every biological process in his body is in a constant state of sujud. His physical existence worships Allah ﷻ every moment.

What’s in rebellion is his unseen heart, his spiritual self, his conscious choice. This understanding must be kept in mind as we read the rest of the ayah.

وَٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ وَٱلنُّجُومُ وَٱلْجِبَالُ وَٱلشَّجَرُ وَٱلدَّوَآبُّ – and the sun prostrates, and the moon, and the stars, and the mountains, and the trees, and all creatures and beasts of every kind.

وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ – and many, so many from among people. These are the ones whose spiritual hearts join their physical bodies in conscious, willing submission.

وَكَثِيرٌ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْعَذَابُ – and many, so many from among people upon whom punishment has become justified, who have made themselves worthy of punishment through their spiritual rebellion even as their bodies remain in submission.

وَمَن يُهِنِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن مُّكْرِمٍ – and whoever Allah decides to humiliate, whoever Allah disgraces, then you will never find anyone capable of bringing honor back to him. No one can restore dignity to the one Allah ﷻ has deemed worthy of humiliation.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَآءُ – Indeed, Allah does whatever He wills. His will is absolute, His wisdom perfect, His judgment final.

This is an ayah of sajdah. When you recite or hear it, you perform a prostration, acknowledging that you are among those who willingly join their bodies and souls in submission to the One who created all things.

Ayah 19–29 · linked video: 04. Al-Haj (Ayah 19-29)

Surah Al-Hajj: Ayahs 19-29

Ayah 19

۞ هَـٰذَانِ خَصْمَانِ ٱخْتَصَمُوا۟ فِى رَبِّهِمْ ۖ فَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ قُطِّعَتْ لَهُمْ ثِيَابٌ مِّن نَّارٍ يُصَبُّ مِن فَوْقِ رُءُوسِهِمُ ٱلْحَمِيمُ

This ayah opens with a powerful declaration: “These two are very argumentative groups” - هَـٰذَانِ خَصْمَانِ. The word خَصْمَانِ comes from the root that describes extremely aggressive argumentation. What makes this particularly striking is that they are arguing “about their Master” - فِى رَبِّهِمْ.

Consider what this means. When you argue about someone with authority over you, there’s an implicit assumption that the person isn’t listening. Think of employees at a lunch table, speaking freely and critically about their boss - but only because they believe he’s not present. The moment they suspect he might overhear them, their entire demeanor changes. The color drains from their faces. The aggressive tone disappears.

This is precisely the issue with theological arguments that lack proper regard. When philosophy professors debate whether God can create a boulder so heavy He cannot lift it, or when people engage in discussions that treat divine attributes as mere intellectual exercises, their confidence reveals a deeper assumption: they are certain no one is listening. They proceed as if there is no possibility of an Entity infinitely more powerful than themselves who might be present and even offended by their discourse.

This extends even to debates among Muslims. When we argue about theology - whether about Allah’s attributes, His names, or matters of creed - we must maintain proper adab. These discussions arose partly from exposure to Greek philosophy and other intellectual traditions, but the Quran itself doesn’t center religion around such debates. The question of where Allah ﷻ is, for example, appears in passing but isn’t a foundational teaching. Yet when Muslims from different theological schools argue their positions, sometimes in their zeal to win the argument, they say things about Allah ﷻ that lack proper reverence. We show tremendous respect when discussing the Prophet ﷺ, and we should show even greater care when speaking about our Master.

The consequence for those who engage in such disrespectful argumentation is devastating: قُطِّعَتْ لَهُمْ ثِيَابٌ مِّن نَّارٍ - “garments of fire have been cut out for them.” The word قُطِّعَتْ indicates tailoring, as if these coverings of flame were specifically designed and fitted for them. But the torment doesn’t end with these garments.

يُصَبُّ مِن فَوْقِ رُءُوسِهِمُ ٱلْحَمِيمُ - “boiling water is poured over their heads.” The verb يُصَبُّ doesn’t describe a gentle pour. This is the kind of violent pouring where massive quantities of liquid overwhelm someone - like when championship-winning coaches get drenched with coolers of sports drink, or like a flood wave crashing down. It’s poured مِن فَوْقِ رُءُوسِهِمْ - from above their heads, completely overwhelming them.

Picture the scene: someone is on fire, desperately asking for water. The water comes - but it’s الْحَمِيمُ, scorching hot water that only adds to their agony.

Ayah 20

يُصْهَرُ بِهِۦ مَا فِى بُطُونِهِمْ وَٱلْجُلُودُ

The horror intensifies. يُصْهَرُ means “it melts” - this boiling water will melt whatever is in their bellies. The fat, the contents, everything internal will be liquefied by this torment. وَٱلْجُلُودُ - “and the skins” - not to mention what happens to their external form.

Ayah 21

وَلَهُم مَّقَـٰمِعُ مِنْ حَدِيدٍ

On top of all this burning and melting, وَلَهُم مَّقَـٰمِعُ مِنْ حَدِيدٍ - “they will have hammers of iron.” These are maces, sledgehammers, whips - implements of iron specifically for their punishment, adding physical beating to the thermal torture.

Ayah 22

كُلَّمَآ أَرَادُوٓا۟ أَن يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنْهَا مِنْ غَمٍّ أُعِيدُوا۟ فِيهَا وَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ

Perhaps the most crushing aspect of this punishment is its inescapability. كُلَّمَآ أَرَادُوٓا۟ أَن يَخْرُجُوا۟ - “Every time they want to get out” - مِنْ غَمٍّ, because of the overwhelming darkness, the hopelessness, the despair - أُعِيدُوا۟ فِيهَا - “they are forced back into it.” There is no exit, no reprieve.

And the command comes: وَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ - “Taste the punishment of the burning flame.” The word الْحَرِيقِ describes a fire that leaves absolutely nothing behind.

Ayah 23

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ يُحَلَّوْنَ فِيهَا مِنْ أَسَاوِرَ مِن ذَهَبٍ وَلُؤْلُؤًا ۖ وَلِبَاسُهُمْ فِيهَا حَرِيرٌ

If that horrifying description doesn’t appeal to you, listen to the alternative. Allah ﷻ reiterates what He has mentioned before, but the contrast makes it even more powerful: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ - “Indeed, Allah will enter those who believed and did righteous deeds” - there is no doubt about this - جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ - “into gardens beneath which rivers flow.”

The image here is magnificent. These gardens sit atop hills, with waterfalls cascading down, and at the bottom, rivers gush and flow. The scene includes the visual and auditory beauty of rushing water, the elevation of paradise, and the constant movement of these rivers.

يُحَلَّوْنَ فِيهَا - “They will be adorned in it.” The word comes from حِلْيَة, meaning jewelry and decoration. مِنْ أَسَاوِرَ مِن ذَهَبٍ وَلُؤْلُؤًا - “with bracelets of gold and pearls.” Some might think, “I’m not interested in wearing gold bracelets and pearls - that doesn’t appeal to me.”

But consider: people today wear expensive watches. Those are bracelets of gold. They wear cufflinks, lapel pins, and other accessories - those are forms of adornment. Expensive shirts worth hundreds of dollars have a fine texture and a certain gloss to them. The adornment of paradise won’t make you look like you raided the costume department of a Broadway production. It’s going to be genuinely beautiful, sophisticated fashion.

وَلِبَاسُهُمْ فِيهَا حَرِيرٌ - “And their garments will be of silk.” Some scholars mention that the outer layer will be fine silk and the inner layer will be thick, luxurious silk.

Ayah 24

وَهُدُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلطَّيِّبِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ وَهُدُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ

But the material blessings aren’t the essence of paradise. وَهُدُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلطَّيِّبِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ - “They were guided to the good and pure speech.” What is this pure speech? It’s the speech that encapsulates the reality of our faith.

These people were guided to statements like “La ilaha illa Allah,” “Alhamdulillah,” “SubhanAllah,” “Allahu Akbar.” Think about the remarkable nature of our religion: the deepest theological truths are contained in phrases that even a child can memorize. In other civilizations and religions, you must reach advanced stages of learning before accessing the profound texts that explain the depths of theology and faith. But in Islam, our deepest theology is embedded in “La ilaha illa Allah, Muhammadur Rasulullah” and “SubhanAllah wa bihamdihi, SubhanAllah al-Azim.”

The phrases of the Quran are so common, so frequently repeated, that even the disbelievers memorized them. They couldn’t help but remember إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ - “Indeed, Allah is aware of what you do.” How many times have we heard that? Yet each of these repeated phrases is essentially a complete summary of Islam. “Allah ﷻ knows what you’re doing” - that alone is enough to transform how you live your life.

وَهُدُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ - “And they were guided to the path of the One who is praised.” الْحَمِيدِ is the One who is inherently praiseworthy, who doesn’t need our praise. When you walk on the path of someone who doesn’t need you, you become humble. You realize: He doesn’t need me. I need Him. This path naturally leads to humility, to recognizing that Allah ﷻ is Al-Hamid - praised in and of Himself, regardless of whether we acknowledge it.

Ayah 25

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلْنَـٰهُ لِلنَّاسِ سَوَآءً ٱلْعَـٰكِفُ فِيهِ وَٱلْبَادِ ۚ وَمَن يُرِدْ فِيهِ بِإِلْحَادٍۭ بِظُلْمٍ نُّذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ

Now Allah ﷻ shifts to a specific crime: إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - “Indeed, those who disbelieved and obstruct from the path of Allah.” The verb يَصُدُّونَ means they both stopped themselves and prevented others - they obstructed both their own path and blocked others from accessing it.

وَٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ - “and from Al-Masjid al-Haram.” What does this mean? They prevented themselves from doing what’s right at the sacred house, and they prevented others from doing the same. This house that was established for the worship of One God, they transformed into a regional center for shirk.

ٱلَّذِى جَعَلْنَـٰهُ لِلنَّاسِ سَوَآءً - “Which We made for all people equally.” The house was meant to be a place of no discrimination. Ibrahim (AS) was commanded to make the announcement, and everyone from everywhere was supposed to be able to come and worship Allah ﷻ there.

Consider when these ayahs were being revealed. The Prophet ﷺ was making hijrah, leaving Makkah. The believers were traveling away from the Kaaba, their backs to the sacred house, and they were hearing these words. Allah ﷻ is acknowledging: “I know what you’re going through. I know what they did to you. I know they kicked you out. I know their crimes. And I know that house was supposed to be for all peoples.”

ٱلْعَـٰكِفُ فِيهِ وَٱلْبَادِ - “The resident therein and the visitor from outside.” العَاكِفُ is someone who moved to Makkah specifically because of the spiritual greatness they felt near the Kaaba. They stayed there due to the religious significance of the place. وَٱلْبَادِ refers to those who come from البَادِيَة - the distant, rural areas where there aren’t many buildings. The Bedouins, the people from far-flung regions.

This is significant because Makkah wasn’t a huge city at that time. The locals knew each other, and they could easily identify foreigners. Yet the tradition was supposed to be that everyone - local resident and distant traveler alike - should be treated equally once they entered the sacred precincts. And with few exceptions, this remains true today. Despite all the class distinctions and social hierarchies in the region, when you’re inside the Haram, for the most part, you aren’t treated differently. Of course, there are exceptions - when dignitaries visit or when royalty wants to visit the Kaaba and security clears everyone out. But the principle remains.

وَمَن يُرِدْ فِيهِ بِإِلْحَادٍۭ بِظُلْمٍ نُّذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ - “And whoever intends in it deviation with wrongdoing, We will make him taste painful punishment.”

The word إِلْحَاد comes from لَحْد, which refers to a grave that was supposed to be dug as a rectangle but came out crooked - it deviated from the proper form. So إِلْحَاد is any deviation from the straight path. When someone kicks people out of the Haram or wrongfully denies them access, they are deviating from what this house was meant to be, and they are committing ظُلْم - wrongdoing, injustice. The punishment for this is severe and painful.

Ayah 26

وَإِذْ بَوَّأْنَا لِإِبْرَٰهِيمَ مَكَانَ ٱلْبَيْتِ أَن لَّا تُشْرِكْ بِى شَيْـًٔا وَطَهِّرْ بَيْتِىَ لِلطَّآئِفِينَ وَٱلْقَآئِمِينَ وَٱلرُّكَّعِ ٱلسُّجُودِ

Now, speaking of this house and the tradition that should not be deviated from, let’s examine where this tradition originated. وَإِذْ بَوَّأْنَا لِإِبْرَٰهِيمَ مَكَانَ ٱلْبَيْتِ - “And when We settled Ibrahim in the location of the House.”

The word بَوَّأْنَا is remarkable. تَبَوُّأ in Arabic describes moving from one place to another, but with a specific nuance: everything about the new location is perfect. You check the view, the neighbors, the street, the crime rate, the school district, your commute to work, shopping access, utility costs, tax rates - every single factor. When everything is exactly as you want it and you move there comfortably, that’s تَبَوُّأ. Allah ﷻ gave Ibrahim (AS) the perfect place for this house, the perfect location in every sense.

Now look around at that location at the time. There was virtually nothing there. First, Ibrahim (AS) settled his family in that barren valley, and eventually he returned with his son to build the Kaaba.

The phrase مَكَانَ ٱلْبَيْتِ - “the location of the House” - suggests something significant. It indicates the house already had some foundation there. This aligns with the understanding that Adam (AS) may have built the original structure, and some foundational elements remained. Allah ﷻ guided Ibrahim (AS) precisely to where he was supposed to be, to refurbish and rebuild that house.

This is why the Quran says, “وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ ٱلْقَوَاعِدَ مِنَ ٱلْبَيْتِ” - “And when Ibrahim raised the foundations of the House.” It doesn’t say he placed the foundations down; he raised them. The foundations were already there, waiting to be elevated and built upon again.

أَن لَّا تُشْرِكْ بِى شَيْـًٔا - “That you not associate anything with Me.” The very first command regarding this house: don’t do shirk with Me in any way, shape or form.

وَطَهِّرْ بَيْتِىَ - “And purify My house.” Notice Allah ﷻ says “My house” - بَيْتِى. This establishes ownership and sanctity.

لِلطَّآئِفِينَ وَٱلْقَآئِمِينَ وَٱلرُّكَّعِ ٱلسُّجُودِ - “For those who perform Tawaf, and those who stand, and those who bow and prostrate.” Purify it for those who will circle the Kaaba, and for those who will stand in prayer. Notice there’s no “and” between الرُّكَّعِ ٱلسُّجُودِ - those who bow and those who prostrate - because ruku and sujud are together, they’re part of one unified action, which is salah.

Ayah 27

وَأَذِّن فِى ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْحَجِّ يَأْتُوكَ رِجَالًا وَعَلَىٰ كُلِّ ضَامِرٍ يَأْتِينَ مِن كُلِّ فَجٍّ عَمِيقٍ

وَأَذِّن فِى ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْحَجِّ - “And make an announcement among the people about the Hajj.” Allah ﷻ commanded Ibrahim (AS) to proclaim the pilgrimage, to let it be heard among humanity.

يَأْتُوكَ رِجَالًا - “They will come to you on foot.” The word رِجَالًا here doesn’t mean “as men” in the sense of male versus female. It means walking, on foot, by land.

وَعَلَىٰ كُلِّ ضَامِرٍ - “And on every lean camel.” ضَامِر describes an animal that’s skinny, but skinny for a specific reason. The journey to Makkah is so long that even a fat, well-fed camel becomes lean by the time it arrives. Or ضَامِر can mean lean and muscular from intense exercise - these animals get quite a workout making the journey. They become fit from the exertion.

يَأْتِينَ مِن كُلِّ فَجٍّ عَمِيقٍ - “They come from every distant mountain pass.” فَجّ refers to valleys and open roads between mountains. عَمِيق means deep, far out, from the most remote places imaginable.

Now imagine this announcement being made by Ibrahim (AS) thousands of years ago. Allah ﷻ carried that announcement and entered it into the hearts of people across time and space. To this day, there are waiting lists for Hajj that span years. Countries have passport quotas because the demand is so overwhelming.

When you go to Hajj, you’ll meet Muslims from places you never knew existed. Someone will say, “I’m from this island,” and you’ve never heard the name of the island. “How many Muslims are on your island?” “Actually, fifty.” “And I’m one of them. I’m supposed to be the imam. I’m learning how to lead prayer this year, then I’ll go back and teach the rest.” This is مِن كُلِّ فَجٍّ عَمِيقٍ manifesting before your eyes.

Ayah 28

لِّيَشْهَدُوا۟ مَنَـٰفِعَ لَهُمْ وَيَذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ فِىٓ أَيَّامٍ مَّعْلُومَـٰتٍ عَلَىٰ مَا رَزَقَهُم مِّنۢ بَهِيمَةِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ ۖ فَكُلُوا۟ مِنْهَا وَأَطْعِمُوا۟ ٱلْبَآئِسَ ٱلْفَقِيرَ

لِّيَشْهَدُوا۟ مَنَـٰفِعَ لَهُمْ - “So that they may witness benefits for themselves.” There are multiple benefits to this journey. On the way, they might discover business opportunities, meet people from other lands, find new places to settle. And of course, they’ll find the spiritual benefits when they arrive at the sacred house.

وَيَذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ فِىٓ أَيَّامٍ مَّعْلُومَـٰتٍ - “And that they may mention the name of Allah during known days.” Some scholars say this refers specifically to mentioning Allah’s name as they sacrifice animals during the days of sacrifice.

عَلَىٰ مَا رَزَقَهُم مِّنۢ بَهِيمَةِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ - “Over what He has provided them of grazing livestock.” بَهِيمَةِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ refers to four-legged cattle - camels, cows, sheep, goats. You’re meant to sacrifice these animals at Hajj, and as you do so, you pronounce the name of Allah ﷻ.

فَكُلُوا۟ مِنْهَا - “So eat from it.” You’re permitted to eat from the meat you sacrifice.

وَأَطْعِمُوا۟ ٱلْبَآئِسَ ٱلْفَقِيرَ - “And feed the distressed poor.” الْبَآئِس describes someone in a critical, desperate situation. This desperation can happen for multiple reasons. Remember, it’s meat you’re dealing with. You can’t travel back to Syria or Yemen on foot with meat and arrive five months later expecting it to still be good. So you must give it to people in the area.

But there’s another dimension: Hajj attracts enormous crowds. Someone might have lost their way. Someone might have had their money or documents stolen. They could be in a genuinely difficult situation - that’s بَائِس. It describes intense hardship, whether from extreme hunger or from being caught in a desperate circumstance. Make sure you feed those who are in genuine need.

Ayah 29

ثُمَّ لْيَقْضُوا۟ تَفَثَهُمْ وَلْيُوفُوا۟ نُذُورَهُمْ وَلْيَطَّوَّفُوا۟ بِٱلْبَيْتِ ٱلْعَتِيقِ

ثُمَّ لْيَقْضُوا۟ تَفَثَهُمْ - “Then let them do away with their untidiness.” تَفَث refers to the dirt, the grime, the state of being unkempt. Hajj is physically exhausting. You’re covered in dust and dirt. You haven’t showered properly. Your nails have grown long. Your hair is overgrown. You haven’t groomed yourself.

Now that the major rites are complete, it’s time to clean up. Freshen up. Take a proper shower. Trim your nails. Cut your hair. Put on perfume or cologne. Groom yourself and dress nicely. That entire process of becoming clean and presentable - that’s what’s captured in the phrase لْيَقْضُوا۟ تَفَثَهُمْ.

وَلْيُوفُوا۟ نُذُورَهُمْ - “And let them fulfill their vows.” نَذْر is when you devote something to Allah ﷻ, when you make a pledge or commitment. Whatever promises you made to Allah ﷻ, whatever you vowed to do - fulfill those pledges.

وَلْيَطَّوَّفُوا۟ بِٱلْبَيْتِ ٱلْعَتِيقِ - “And let them perform Tawaf around the Ancient House.”

الْعَتِيق is a beautiful and profound word for the Kaaba. It comes from عِتْق, which means to be pure in origin, ancient, and free. This has multiple meanings.

First, it means the house wasn’t something else that got converted. It’s not like a masjid that used to be a church, or a church that used to be an office building. You see this sometimes - a millionaire’s estate gets sold and converted into a masjid, or an old office building becomes a place of worship. Those places weren’t originally what they are now; they’ve been restructured and repurposed. The Kaaba is different. It is originally what it was meant to be. It has always been the House of Allah ﷻ.

Second, الْعَتِيق describes something that is very old but doesn’t lose its sanctity or value over time. When most buildings get old and weathered, people abandon them. They lose their worth. Historic buildings require entire committees and councils to protect, renovate and maintain them, and even then, many people in the community don’t care about preserving them. They’d rather have new commercial buildings or apartments.

But if a place is ancient and its value doesn’t diminish with time - if it continues to be cherished and its sanctity keeps increasing - that’s عَتِيق. The Kaaba has stood for millennia, and every generation values it more than the last. Its sanctity has never decreased. This is the Ancient House, the house that time cannot diminish, and it is around this blessed structure that the pilgrims complete their Hajj.

Surah Al-Hajj: Ayahs 30-35

Ayah 30

ذَٰلِكَ وَمَن يُعَظِّمْ حُرُمَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُۥ عِندَ رَبِّهِۦ ۗ وَأُحِلَّتْ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمُ إِلَّا مَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ ٱلرِّجْسَ مِنَ ٱلْأَوْثَـٰنِ وَٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ قَوْلَ ٱلزُّورِ

The ayah begins with ذَٰلِكَ, which serves as more than just a simple demonstrative. In Arabic, this expression carries the weight of “Did you get all that? Take that seriously.” It’s a linguistic pause that demands the listener’s attention to what has just been conveyed.

Allah ﷻ then establishes a profound principle: whoever magnifies and makes a big deal out of the حُرُمَـٰتِ - the sanctified prohibitions of Allah ﷻ - this will be better for him with his Master. The word hurumat encompasses everything Allah ﷻ has made sacred and forbidden, especially during Hajj. These include not being mean to others, not yelling, not losing your temper, not hurting anyone, not cursing, refraining from intimacy, and not sacrificing animals before their prescribed time. Your behavior must reflect the sanctity of the occasion. Certain places become restricted, and certain actions become impermissible until the proper time arrives.

The concept of حُرُمَـٰت extends beyond mere prohibition. The root carries connotations of respect and honor - this is why we say مُحْتَرَم for someone deserving respect, like an elder. Every older person has a level of respect that must be honored. When you have the opportunity to go to Hajj, you must leave behind any casual attitude you might have developed. You need to make a big deal out of the Kaaba. The tendency to be unimpressed - to look at it as just a cube, as unremarkable architecture - is unacceptable to Allah ﷻ. Whoever truly magnifies the sanctified places and prohibitions of Allah ﷻ will find that this attitude serves him well in the sight of his Master.

The ayah continues: وَأُحِلَّتْ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمُ إِلَّا مَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ (And the cattle have been made lawful for you, except what has been recited to you). This reminder references the dietary laws already revealed to Muslims, even in the early Makkan or Madinan period, specifying which animals are permissible and which are not.

Then comes a crucial command: فَٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ ٱلرِّجْسَ مِنَ ٱلْأَوْثَـٰنِ (So avoid the filth of idols). The word وَثَن is distinct from صَنَم. While sanam refers to manufactured, carved idols, wathan refers to ancient statues that have remained in place for long periods. This category also includes objects of worship that aren’t carved at all - a sacred rock, a holy tree, or water from a revered river that people consider too sacred to even carve. These natural objects that become objects of veneration fall under the category of awthan.

The command to avoid these carries special significance because statues had been integral to Makkan tradition for centuries. The Sahaba (RA) who embraced Islam had been raised in this environment. These associations were almost subconscious, deeply embedded in their cultural memory. Allah ﷻ goes out of His way to warn them that when they go to make Hajj, they must ensure they don’t give any sanctity to these things or speak about them in the old ways.

One convert from Hinduism exemplified this sensitivity. After embracing Islam, despite loving action figures as a hobby he shared with his child, he made a deliberate point of keeping such figures on the ground where they could be kicked around, ensuring they would never be elevated or honored. When asked about this, he explained that he felt compelled to do this because of his past life immersed in idolatry. This is the kind of sensitivity Allah ﷻ wanted to instill in the Sahaba (RA) who had lived lives surrounded by shirk.

The ayah concludes with another command: وَٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ قَوْلَ ٱلزُّورِ (And avoid false speech). The word زُور encompasses lies, falsehood, and speech that sidesteps or speaks against the truth. This prohibition is mentioned here because when people used to go to Hajj during the time of ignorance, they would revive and review the mythologies of shirk, recounting stories that glorified their idols. New Muslims, though they had accepted Islam, had been raised on these narratives. Sometimes these stories would emerge almost subconsciously. Allah ﷻ wanted to ensure that when Muslims went to make Hajj, they would guard their tongues from giving any credence to these false traditions.

What’s remarkable about these verses is their deeper implication. Allah ﷻ is giving the Muslims instructions about performing Hajj - avoiding idols, sacrificing animals properly, observing the major rituals. But remember, they’re leaving Makkah. Where do you perform Hajj? In Makkah. The Quraysh had just kicked them out and nearly killed them all. Yet Allah ﷻ speaks with certainty about their return: Your return to Makkah is guaranteed. Don’t worry about that. You’re absolutely coming back.

Ayah 31

حُنَفَآءَ لِلَّهِ غَيْرَ مُشْرِكِينَ بِهِۦ ۚ وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَكَأَنَّمَا خَرَّ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَتَخْطَفُهُ ٱلطَّيْرُ أَوْ تَهْوِى بِهِ ٱلرِّيحُ فِى مَكَانٍ سَحِيقٍ

Allah ﷻ describes the state Muslims should embody when performing Hajj: حُنَفَآءَ لِلَّهِ - solely dedicated to Allah ﷻ, focused entirely on Him with no other distraction. The word حَنِيف captures a concept of being purely inclined toward something, with no bias pulling you in another direction. You are completely focused. This means during Hajj, Muslims shouldn’t be thinking about anybody else or worrying about anything else. Don’t worry about updating social media. Don’t worry about building a photo library. This isn’t a visit to a tourist destination or a national park. You went there to meet Allah ﷻ. That’s the purpose of your journey.

The command continues: غَيْرَ مُشْرِكِينَ بِهِۦ (not committing any shirk with Him whatsoever). This reinforces that Hajj is a time of absolute devotion to Allah ﷻ alone. No other concerns should occupy your mind. People sometimes engage in trivial activities - texting each other to make jokes, calling friends to describe the heat, circling the Kaaba while distracted. But you won’t have your cell phone on Judgment Day. Hajj is supposed to be a rehearsal for that Day.

Hajj serves as an international convention that reminds humanity of Judgment Day. Millions of people wearing clothes resembling burial shrouds, having nothing on their backs, coming together responding to Allah’s call - لَبَّيْكَ اللَّهُمَّ لَبَّيْكَ. They gather in the field of Arafah, where all humanity will be gathered on the Day of Resurrection. Everything about Hajj is a rehearsal. On Judgment Day, we’ll be cursing Shaytan and people will be blaming him - just as we do at the Jamarat during Hajj. You leave everything behind, you cannot enjoy the normal pleasures of life. This is preparation for standing before Allah ﷻ.

Then Allah ﷻ presents one of the most powerful images in the Quran: وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَكَأَنَّمَا خَرَّ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ (And whoever commits shirk with Allah, it is as though he has fallen from the sky). This connects to an earlier parable in the surah about the rope extending to the sky. If someone loses hope in Allah ﷻ and cuts that rope, what happens? They fall. That image continues here with even more devastating detail.

The parable continues: فَتَخْطَفُهُ ٱلطَّيْرُ (and the birds snatch him). Picture someone falling from the sky while birds are tearing at him, gnawing at his flesh. Some critics have called this parable illogical, arguing that it’s impossible to imagine someone falling at such high velocity while birds simultaneously attack him. They claim that a good parable should be something the audience can visualize from their experience, and since nobody has witnessed someone falling from the sky being attacked by birds, this fails as an effective illustration.

But this criticism misses the richness of Arabic expression. “Falling from the sky” was a figure of speech used in pre-Islamic Arabia, specifically in military contexts. When soldiers fought from a mountain, holding the high ground, they had the tactical advantage. If despite this advantage, the enemy from below managed to advance, kill the defenders, and throw their corpses off the cliff - this was described as خَرَّ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ (falling from the sky). This phrase carried enormous shame.

Understanding this cultural context is crucial for grasping the parable’s power. The Arabs who first heard this ayah didn’t believe in the afterlife. For them, death was the end. Since they had no concept of eternal consequences, how could they understand the gravity of shirk? Their worst fear wasn’t hellfire - they didn’t believe in it. Their worst fear was dying in a humiliating way that would make people mock their memory for generations.

If you don’t believe in an afterlife, what lives on after you? Only your name and reputation. Wealthy non-believers approaching death often donate to hospitals or universities so buildings can be named after them - desperate for some form of immortality. For the Arabs, immortality came through heroic death in battle, through poetry sung about their valor. The worst nightmare was to be remembered as “that guy who fell from the sky” - the warrior so pathetic that enemies climbed up, killed him, and threw his body off the cliff in humiliation. Such a person would be mocked for centuries as either a defeated loser or a coward who jumped rather than fight.

The parable continues its devastating imagery: Birds come and gnaw at his corpse. Usually, after battle, both sides collect their dead for proper burial. Even the losing side eventually retrieves their fallen. But this person is such a humiliation to his own people that nobody comes to give him a funeral. Only vultures come, birds known for eating filth. Yet even these scavengers, after having their meal, find what remains too disgusting. They fly away, leaving remnants of bone and flesh.

Then comes another layer of degradation: أَوْ تَهْوِى بِهِ ٱلرِّيحُ فِى مَكَانٍ سَحِيقٍ (or the wind blows him to a remote place). Whatever scum and fragments remain are blown by the wind into the wilderness, into the bush - مَكَانٍ سَحِيقٍ - a place that is crushed, beaten down, far away, unknown and uncharted, where no one will ever find it.

Critics questioned why it matters where the remains end up - whether in civilization or the wilderness, the person is dead either way. But this misses the point entirely. The mention of this remote location adds the final layer of humiliation: complete erasure from memory. Not only was his death shameful, not only did scavengers abandon his corpse, but even his remains disappear into an unknown place where no trace of him will ever be found.

This is how Allah ﷻ illustrates the ugliness of shirk to people who don’t understand the akhirah. From their own worldview, using their own values and fears, He shows them just how degrading shirk truly is. The parable doesn’t require belief in the afterlife to convey horror - it speaks in the language of honor, shame, and legacy that the original audience understood intimately.

Ayah 32

ذَٰلِكَ وَمَن يُعَظِّمْ شَعَـٰٓئِرَ ٱللَّهِ فَإِنَّهَا مِن تَقْوَى ٱلْقُلُوبِ

Again the ayah opens with ذَٰلِكَ - that emphatic “Did you get that?” - marking what has just been explained as crucial to internalize.

Allah ﷻ introduces another dimension of magnification: وَمَن يُعَظِّمْ شَعَـٰٓئِرَ ٱللَّهِ (And whoever magnifies the sha’air of Allah). The word شَعَائِر comes from شَعَرَ, meaning to feel, to be aware, to recognize. The sha’air are those things that raise your awareness of Allah ﷻ, that make you conscious of Him, that jog your spiritual memory.

When you see the Kaaba, you become more aware of Allah ﷻ. When you visit Masjid Al-Haram, when you smell Zamzam water, your Iman increases as memories of the entire Islamic legacy flood your mind. Walking by the battlefield of Badr, you imagine what it must have been like when the Sahaba (RA) stood there. Praying in Masjid An-Nabawi, you envision the Prophet ﷺ leading Salah with the Companions (RA) behind him. These are the sha’air of Allah ﷻ - places and things that bring about recognition of Allah ﷻ, that make you feel closer to Him.

The ayah teaches us: فَإِنَّهَا مِن تَقْوَى ٱلْقُلُوبِ (Indeed, it is from the taqwa of hearts). When someone makes a big deal out of these places, when they visit them with reverence, when they reminisce about the past and feel that closeness to Allah ﷻ - this is a sign that taqwa resides in their hearts. This magnification isn’t superficial; it emerges from genuine God-consciousness dwelling within.

Ayah 33

لَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ثُمَّ مَحِلُّهَآ إِلَى ٱلْبَيْتِ ٱلْعَتِيقِ

This ayah addresses practical matters regarding the animals designated for sacrifice: لَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى (For you in them are benefits until a specified time). You may derive benefit from these animals before they are sacrificed. You can drink their milk, ride them for transportation, use their wool. The designation of an animal for sacrifice doesn’t mean you suddenly cannot benefit from it in permissible ways.

ثُمَّ مَحِلُّهَآ إِلَى ٱلْبَيْتِ ٱلْعَتِيقِ (Then their place of sacrifice is at the Ancient House). The final destination for these animals is the valley of Mina, near the Kaaba - البيت العتيق, the Ancient House. This specifies where the sacrifice must ultimately take place, connecting the ritual to the sacred precincts established by Ibrahim (AS).

Ayah 34

وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ جَعَلْنَا مَنسَكًا لِّيَذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَىٰ مَا رَزَقَهُم مِّنۢ بَهِيمَةِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ ۗ فَإِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ فَلَهُۥٓ أَسْلِمُوا۟ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُخْبِتِينَ

Allah ﷻ reveals a profound historical truth: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ جَعَلْنَا مَنسَكًا (And for every nation We have appointed a rite of sacrifice). The word مَنسَك refers to a place, method, and manner of sacrifice. Each nation throughout history received its own form of sacrifice from Allah ﷻ. The tradition of the Israelites differed from that of the nation of Salih (AS), which differed from others.

This is mentioned because some Christians had begun questioning the Islamic form of sacrifice, claiming they had never heard of such a practice, suggesting it was merely an Arabian custom. But Allah ﷻ clarifies that this is divinely ordained, not a cultural invention. What’s remarkable is that the Muslim tradition of sacrifice is actually closest to the ancient Arab tradition - not to the Jewish tradition. This might initially seem problematic since Muslims are often viewed as closer to the People of the Book than to the pagan Arabs.

However, this similarity provides powerful evidence for a profound truth: the son destined to be sacrificed by Ibrahim (AS) was Ismail (AS), not Ishaq (AS). When Ismail (AS) settled in Makkah and built the House with his father Ibrahim (AS), the tradition of sacrifice was passed down through his offspring. Over generations and centuries, as his descendants fell into shirk, this pure monotheistic practice became corrupted into a polytheistic tradition. Yet the core ritual remained. When Islam came, it restored that tradition to its original pure form.

So the Arabs had been sacrificing in the way of Ibrahim (AS) for thousands of years, even while not believing in Allah ﷻ as He deserves to be believed in. Where did that tradition originate?
