From Ibrahim and Ismail (AS). The continuity of this practice through millennia of shirk back to its pure form in Islam demonstrates the deep historical roots of this ummah in Makkah.

The purpose is stated clearly: لِّيَذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَىٰ مَا رَزَقَهُم مِّنۢ بَهِيمَةِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ (So that they may mention the name of Allah over what He has provided them of grazing livestock) - sheep, cows, camels. The sacrifice isn’t merely ritual slaughter; it’s an act of remembering Allah ﷻ, acknowledging Him as the Provider, recognizing His blessings.

Then comes a declaration of tawhid: فَإِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ فَلَهُۥٓ أَسْلِمُوا۟ (For your God is One God, so to Him submit). The sacrifice, the Hajj, all these rituals point to one ultimate reality: Allah ﷻ is One. He alone deserves worship and submission. Therefore, to Him alone you must submit completely.

The ayah concludes with a beautiful command: وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُخْبِتِينَ (And give good news to the mukhbitin). The word مُخْبِت comes from خَبَت, describing soft land that becomes damp and yielding after rain. When you step on it, your foot sinks in. Allah ﷻ uses this to describe hearts that have become soft and receptive. There’s a mellowing that happens to people who go through Hajj properly, a softness that enters their personality. Congratulations to those who have become soft-hearted, to those who have allowed Hajj to transform their inner state.

Ayah 35

ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ذُكِرَ ٱللَّهُ وَجِلَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَصَابَهُمْ وَٱلْمُقِيمِى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ

Allah ﷻ now describes these soft-hearted believers in detail. First: ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ذُكِرَ ٱللَّهُ وَجِلَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ (Those who, when Allah is mentioned, their hearts tremble). The mere mention of Allah ﷻ causes their hearts to shake with awe and consciousness. They haven’t become comfortable with sin or casual about their relationship with their Creator. His name evokes genuine reverence within them.

Second: وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَصَابَهُمْ (And those who are patient over whatever befalls them). They bear difficulties with perseverance, accepting trials as part of their journey toward Allah ﷻ. They don’t complain or lose hope when hardship strikes; rather, they maintain their commitment through patience.

Third: وَٱلْمُقِيمِى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ (And those who establish Salah). Here something remarkable occurs in the Arabic. The expected form would be ٱلَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ, but instead it’s shortened to ٱلْمُقِيمِى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ with a special grammatical construction. This shortening, known in Arabic rhetoric as اختصار الكلام, serves to magnify and highlight this group. By using the construct form (mudaf), Allah ﷻ draws special attention to those who truly establish prayer - not merely those who pray, but those who establish it with all its conditions, with presence of heart, with consistency.

Finally: وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (And from what We have provided them, they spend). These believers recognize that everything they possess comes from Allah ﷻ, and they willingly share from what He has given them. Their hearts have become so soft and receptive that they naturally give, understanding that their wealth is a trust and a test.

These five qualities - hearts that tremble at Allah’s remembrance, patience through trials, establishment of prayer, and spending from His provision - characterize the mukhbitin, those whose hearts have been softened through their journey toward Allah ﷻ. May Allah ﷻ make us among them.

Ayah 36–40 · linked video: 06. Al-Haj (Ayah 36-40)

Surah Al-Hajj: Ayahs 36-40

Ayah 36

وَٱلْبُدْنَ جَعَلْنَـٰهَا لَكُم مِّن شَعَـٰٓئِرِ ٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ فِيهَا خَيْرٌ ۖ فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهَا صَوَآفَّ ۖ فَإِذَا وَجَبَتْ جُنُوبُهَا فَكُلُوا۟ مِنْهَا وَأَطْعِمُوا۟ ٱلْقَانِعَ وَٱلْمُعْتَرَّ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ سَخَّرْنَـٰهَا لَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ

The word البُدْن refers to the special camels designated for sacrifice during Hajj. The Arabic language contains numerous words for camels - ibil, badeel, jamal, him, rikkab, naqah, baa’ir, shadd, budn, bahlool, waseelah, and sahibah - reflecting the deep cultural significance of these animals. Just as modern cultures have developed extensive vocabularies for their valued possessions, the Arabs developed a rich lexicon for camels, even creating what we might think of as “brands” to distinguish different types.

The term budn specifically derives from badan, meaning “body,” because these are typically large, well-fed animals selected for sacrifice. Allah ﷻ declares that He has made these animals from among the great signs and reminders of His presence. There is abundant good for you in them, He tells us.

The ayah continues with specific instructions: mention the name of Allah upon them when they are standing in straight rows (صَوَآفَّ). This description comes from the actual practice established by the Prophet ﷺ himself. During his farewell Hajj, he sacrificed 100 camels - 63 of them with his own blessed hands - and they stood in a single line.

The method of sacrificing a camel differs from that of smaller animals like goats. A goat is laid on its side with its legs held, but a camel is far too large and powerful for this approach. Instead, the camel stands upright, its neck is raised, and the sacrifice is performed by making an incision in the neck. As the blood drains, the animal weakens until it tips over to its side - this is what the phrase وَجَبَتْ جُنُوبُهَا refers to, when it becomes mandatory for the animal to fall.

What makes this account truly remarkable is that each camel would come forward and willingly raise its neck for the Prophet ﷺ. These animals could see what had happened to those before them, yet one after another, they presented themselves peacefully. This represents one of the prophetic experiences that no philosopher can adequately explain, no scientist can replicate, and no academic framework can fully contain. Just as the wooden staff (hannaan) that the Prophet ﷺ used to lean on during khutbah began weeping audibly when he stopped using it after the minbar was built - crying like a child because the Prophet ﷺ no longer leaned upon it - these are realities from the realm of belief (iman) that transcend purely rational analysis.

The poet Rumi captured this truth beautifully when he spoke of the weeping stick that no philosopher could ever understand, because these experiences belong exclusively to the prophetic realm that even the most brilliant minds cannot taste or comprehend without faith.

After the sacrifice, Allah ﷻ provides clear guidance about the meat: consume from it yourselves, and feed both القَانِعَ and المُعْتَرَّ. The qaani’ is the person who is content with little, who possesses such self-respect that they never ask for help even when in genuine need. These are the people we must actively seek out - perhaps someone who has been present for a while but you notice hasn’t eaten, someone whose dignity prevents them from requesting assistance. The mu’tarr, on the other hand, is the one desperate enough to overcome their hesitation and ask directly.

The ayah concludes with a profound reminder: كَذَٰلِكَ سَخَّرْنَـٰهَا لَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ - “Thus have We subjected them to you so that you may be grateful.” These massive, powerful beasts have been made subservient to humanity, and this subjugation itself is a mercy deserving our gratitude.

Ayah 37

لَن يَنَالَ ٱللَّهَ لُحُومُهَا وَلَا دِمَآؤُهَا وَلَـٰكِن يَنَالُهُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ مِنكُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ سَخَّرَهَا لَكُمْ لِتُكَبِّرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا هَدَىٰكُمْ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

This ayah captures the spiritual essence that underlies all of Hajj. There is the outward form of Hajj - wearing ihram, performing the rituals, pelting the jamarat, the tawaf - and then there is its inner reality.

Allah ﷻ declares with absolute clarity: لَن يَنَالَ ٱللَّهَ لُحُومُهَا وَلَا دِمَآؤُهَا - “Their flesh will never reach Allah, nor will their blood.” What reaches Him is التَّقْوَىٰ - consciousness of Allah, reverence for Him, awareness of His presence. This is the heart of the matter.

Consider someone who travels to Hajj with wealth earned through haram means, stays in a five-star hotel watching sports on television, barely engages with the rituals, and simply pays someone else to perform the sacrifice on their behalf because they can afford it. Has anything reached Allah ﷻ from such a person? That wasn’t taqwa - perhaps it was a desert cruise, a tour, but it certainly wasn’t the consciousness and devotion that Allah ﷻ seeks from His servants.

This principle extends to all of us, even those unable to perform Hajj. If you fulfill the sunnah of sacrifice by clicking buttons online to have an animal sacrificed in Somalia, Pakistan, or Bangladesh through a relief organization, at minimum cultivate spiritual consciousness of what you’re doing in that moment. You’re not merely spending money - you’re engaging in an act of taqwa, fulfilling the legacy of Ibrahim (AS). We should genuinely love doing this ourselves when possible, bringing that awareness and intentionality to the act.

كَذَٰلِكَ سَخَّرَهَا لَكُمْ لِتُكَبِّرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا هَدَىٰكُمْ - “Thus has He subjected them to you so that you may declare the greatness of Allah for how He has guided you.” When the animal sacrifices are completed, Eid arrives. This marks one of only two occasions in the Quran where takbeer is commanded: at the conclusion of Ramadan and at the conclusion of the Hajj season. This is why we proclaim “Allahu Akbar, Allahu Akbar, Allahu Akbar” when the time arrives.

The guidance (هَدَىٰكُمْ) mentioned here operates on both literal and figurative levels. Figuratively, it means religious guidance - Allah ﷻ has shown you the way. Literally, on the day of Eid itself, you’re meant to take a different physical route to the prayer than the one you took coming home, embodying guidance even in your movements.

وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ - “And give glad tidings to those who excel in goodness (al-muhsineen).” Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn’t say “congratulate the Muslims” - He says congratulate those who have excelled. For those whose Hajj has been accepted by Allah ﷻ, their slate is now completely clean. It cannot be cleaner. And those whose slates are clean possess the best possible relationship with their Lord. Such people have the opportunity now to be from the people of ihsan, of excellence in worship and character. This is truly something worthy of congratulation - an achievement deserving celebration.

Ayah 38

۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدَٰفِعُ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ خَوَّانٍ كَفُورٍ

This ayah provides reassurance to those preparing for Hajj, but it contains an implicit warning about what awaits in Makkah. The Quraysh still control the sacred precincts, and confrontation looms on the horizon.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدَٰفِعُ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ - “Indeed, Allah defends those who believe.” The promise is unequivocal: Allah ﷻ Himself will be your defender. Though you may feel vulnerable, though the enemy appears stronger and better prepared, divine protection surrounds the believers.

The ayah then describes those who oppose the believers with two powerful characteristics: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ خَوَّانٍ كَفُورٍ - “Indeed, Allah does not love every habitual traitor, ungrateful disbeliever.”

The word خَوَّان - with its intensive form - means someone repeatedly dishonest, habitually treacherous. This describes the Quraysh perfectly. Year after year, they corrupted the legacy of Ibrahim (AS), turning the sacred rites into occasions for idolatry and injustice. Their betrayal wasn’t a single act but a pattern of behavior spanning generations.

كَفُور - extremely ungrateful - captures another dimension of their failure. Every blessing, every privilege, every bit of respect the Quraysh enjoyed throughout the Arabian Peninsula came directly from their custodianship of the House of Allah ﷻ. The Kaaba granted them prestige, protection, and prosperity. Yet instead of gratitude, they responded with arrogance and oppression. They are khawwaan in their repeated dishonesty and kafoor in their profound ingratitude.

Ayah 39

أُذِنَ لِلَّذِينَ يُقَـٰتَلُونَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ظُلِمُوا۟ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ نَصْرِهِمْ لَقَدِيرٌ

This ayah marks a monumental shift in Islamic history: أُذِنَ - “Permission is granted.” The passive voice itself is significant - permission is being granted from the highest authority.

لِلَّذِينَ يُقَـٰتَلُونَ - “To those who are being fought against.” Notice the phrasing: those who are fought against, not those who are fighting. The believers are not the aggressors; they are the ones being attacked, persecuted, driven from their homes.

بِأَنَّهُمْ ظُلِمُوا۟ - “Because they have been wronged.” The justification is crystal clear - this permission comes solely because of the injustice inflicted upon them.

The profound treasure hidden in the word أُذِنَ (permission is granted) is what it reveals about everything that came before. If permission is now being granted, it means that until this moment, permission did not exist. Throughout the entire Makkan period - through all the torture, persecution, economic boycott, and suffering - the Muslims were explicitly forbidden from fighting back.

Other places in the Quran make this clear: كُفُّوا أَيْدِيَكُمْ - “Hold back your hands. Take a defensive stance.” This was the Makkan policy, and it was absolutely critical to the success of that phase of the prophetic mission.

Why couldn’t they fight during the Makkan period? These weren’t incapable people - they had fought in battles before, they knew how to defend themselves. But when you are working on da’wah, when your primary mission is delivering the message of Islam, the physical policy must be one of patience. Think of Prophet Yusuf (AS) - فَصَبْرٌ جَمِيلٌ - beautiful patience. This wasn’t weakness; it was strategic spiritual discipline.

If even one Muslim had responded violently during the Makkan period, every allegation against them would have been validated. The Quraysh were already saying, “These people are a threat to our nation, don’t listen to their message.” One act of violence would have given them exactly the proof they needed: “See? We told you they were dangerous! If one of them did it, all of them can do it. We should eliminate all of them.”

This is precisely the mechanism by which Muslims are demonized today. One act of violence, one person or group acting in a certain way, and everybody else gets thrown under the bus because people were already looking for an excuse. The Prophet ﷺ understood this. He refused to equip his enemies with that media arsenal.

Every Muslim watching news of tragedies today experiences that same anxiety: “Please don’t let it be a Muslim.” One senseless act of violence from someone claiming Islam and the entire message becomes disrupted. Try to talk to anyone about Islam afterward - the first question becomes about terrorism, about violence. One act can dismantle decades of patient work.

The Prophet ﷺ understood what we call “soft power” long before modern political science coined the term. Passive revolutions, nonviolent resistance movements - we’ve seen examples in recent history: Gandhi’s movement against colonialism, Tiananmen Square, the initial Arab Spring uprisings. But the first major example of successful peaceful protest in world history was the Makkan period of the prophetic mission.

This wasn’t about civil rights in the conventional sense. Most conflicts in the Muslim world today center on civil rights - homes being invaded, lands being usurped, people fighting for their property and dignity. Those are legitimate grievances with their own scholarly discussions. But the prophetic mission was different. These early Muslims were tortured for one reason only: they decided to share the message of Islam with their own people. Their own fellow citizens chose to strip away their civil rights, and the Muslims’ response was: “You can do whatever you want to us. Actually, the more you torture us, the more we will intensify our message.”

This became its own form of da’wah. The youth, the weak, the oppressed would witness these believers enduring beatings for their message and ask: “What is this message worth dying for?” They would come and listen, and they would hear: Don’t bury baby girls alive. Wealthy businessmen shouldn’t cheat the poor. These weren’t merely theological abstractions - this was social justice without raising a single hand in violence. This was an act of immense courage.

This dimension of the prophetic example deserves far more discussion in our circles than it receives. The role of any Muslim community in the world today should be to serve as the voice of justice and to spread the word of Allah ﷻ. If we get in trouble, it should be for that reason - no other. Every prophet in history faced trials for spreading Allah’s message. If we face trials for other reasons, perhaps Allah ﷻ is using those difficulties to wake us up, to redirect us toward our true purpose.

Muslims will face trials either way - but those trials either come because you’re doing the right thing, or they come because Allah ﷻ is using His discipline to push you toward doing the right thing.

Now, with this ayah, the permission is finally granted: يُقَـٰتَلُونَ - those who are being fought against may now defend themselves.

وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ نَصْرِهِمْ لَقَدِيرٌ - “And indeed, Allah is completely capable of granting them victory.” The believers might worry: “How can we fight them? We’re not ready. They’re well-armed, they have a prepared army, we don’t even have weapons or a military budget. How are we supposed to take them on?” Allah ﷻ responds: I Myself am completely capable of helping these people who were wronged. Your apparent weakness is irrelevant when divine support is promised.

Ayah 40

ٱلَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِم بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ إِلَّآ أَن يَقُولُوا۟ رَبُّنَا ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ ٱللَّهِ ٱلنَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّهُدِّمَتْ صَوَٰمِعُ وَبِيَعٌ وَصَلَوَٰتٌ وَمَسَـٰجِدُ يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا ٱسْمُ ٱللَّهِ كَثِيرًا ۗ وَلَيَنصُرَنَّ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَنصُرُهُۥٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَقَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌ

Allah ﷻ now describes these believers with profound clarity: ٱلَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِم بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ - “Those who were expelled from their homes without any right, without any justification.”

إِلَّآ أَن يَقُولُوا۟ رَبُّنَا ٱللَّهُ - “For no other reason except that they said: Our Lord is Allah.” This is the only “crime” they committed. They didn’t steal, didn’t attack anyone, didn’t seize property. They simply declared the truth about their Creator.

The ayah then unveils a universal principle that extends far beyond the immediate historical context: وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ ٱللَّهِ ٱلنَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ - “Had Allah not checked one set of people by means of another…”

What follows is remarkable. Allah ﷻ lists places of worship that would have been destroyed: لَّهُدِّمَتْ صَوَٰمِعُ - monasteries would have been demolished. The word صَوَامِعُ refers to places where devoted worship occurs, often secluded locations of spiritual retreat.

وَبِيَعٌ - churches would have been destroyed. The word بِيَع refers to Christian houses of worship.

وَصَلَوَٰتٌ - synagogues would have been destroyed. The Quran uses صَلَوَات for synagogues rather than the Hebrew term “kaniset” (house of assembly), because salawat emphasizes what people gather for - prayer - rather than merely the fact of gathering.

وَمَسَـٰجِدُ - and mosques would have been destroyed.

This sequence is profound. Allah ﷻ is establishing a principle of religious pluralism and divine balance. If one nation or group were given absolute, unchecked power, it would oppress and usurp the rights of other religions. The system Allah ﷻ has established involves checking power through power, preventing any single group from achieving total dominance that leads to the destruction of others’ sacred spaces.

يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا ٱسْمُ ٱللَّهِ كَثِيرًا - “In which the name of Allah is mentioned abundantly.” This phrase applies to all the places of worship mentioned, acknowledging that in monasteries, churches, synagogues, and mosques, the name of God is remembered and glorified.

وَلَيَنصُرَنَّ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَنصُرُهُۥٓ - “And Allah will surely support those who support Him.” This is one of the most powerful principles in Islam, and Allah ﷻ swears to its truth.

What does it mean to support Allah ﷻ? We find the answer in the example of the Ansar - the Helpers of Madinah. When Prophet Isa (AS) asked, “Who will be my helpers toward Allah?” and received no response, the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ asked the same question and the Ansar responded: نَحْنُ أَنصَارُ اللَّهِ - “We are the helpers of Allah.” They understood that helping the Prophet ﷺ in his mission meant helping Allah’s cause.

What is that mission? To deliver this message to the world, to let every human being who breathes on this earth know that Muhammad ﷺ is the Messenger of Allah ﷻ, that this is the final revelation meant for all of humanity. When you take on that responsibility, when you truly commit to helping Allah’s religion, Allah’s guarantee is absolute: وَلَيَنصُرَنَّ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَنصُرُهُۥٓ - He will support you.

This raises the critical question for our times: when we look around and wonder why Allah’s help doesn’t seem to be manifesting, we must ask ourselves - are we truly helping Allah’s religion? Has a critical mass of Muslims committed themselves to genuinely supporting His cause? Until we reach that threshold, we cannot expect the fullness of divine support.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَقَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌ - “Indeed, Allah is the All-Powerful, the All-Mighty.” He is قَوِىّ - possessed of ultimate strength - and عَزِيزٌ - the ultimate authority Who commands absolute respect and can never be overcome. With these two Names, Allah ﷻ concludes this passage, reminding us that regardless of how powerful the opposition appears, the true power and authority belong exclusively to Him.

Ayah 41–53 · linked video: 07. Al-Haj (Ayah 41-53)

Surah Al-Hajj: Ayahs 41-53

Ayah 41

ٱلَّذِينَ إِن مَّكَّنَّـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَوُا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَمَرُوا۟ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَنَهَوْا۟ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ ۗ وَلِلَّهِ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْأُمُورِ

This ayah presents something remarkable in the Quran: an incomplete sentence left for us to complete with our hearts. This literary device appears throughout the Quran, sometimes for praise and sometimes for condemnation. When someone is so disappointed or so impressed that they cannot finish their sentence, they leave the thought hanging. Imagine a teacher saying, “The students who didn’t even finish their exam…” and then trailing off. The silence speaks louder than any words could. The disappointment is too deep to articulate. Similarly, praise can be left incomplete: “The students who got perfect scores on every test…” What happened to them? The excellence is beyond what words can capture.

Allah ﷻ uses this same technique here: “Those people who, if We had settled them in the land, given them stability in the earth…” What follows are their characteristics: they would establish the prayer, give the zakat, command what is right, and forbid what is wrong. Then the sentence ends with “and to Allah belongs the final outcome of all matters.” But there’s an intentional gap, a space left open for our imagination.

Why this incomplete ending? Because Allah ﷻ is so pleased with people who, after being given stability and a comfortable lifestyle, immediately turn to establishing certain institutions in their community. What are these institutions? First, the establishment of prayer - not just individual prayer, but prayer as a community institution. Second, the systematic giving of zakat. Third, ensuring that good is constantly encouraged and promoted. Fourth, ensuring that wrong is constantly spoken against and resisted.

When a community accomplishes this, they are so praiseworthy that the reward is left to our imagination. Allah ﷻ is essentially saying: “For such people, what I will do as a reward is beyond description.”

This has powerful relevance for Muslims living in stable societies today. Despite political rhetoric and challenges, many Muslim communities, particularly in places like the United States, enjoy remarkable blessings. We have resources, talent, educated people, and freedom to speak, write, produce, and establish institutions that can truly make a difference. We have opportunities that many Muslim communities around the world do not have.

The first institution Allah ﷻ wants us to build is إقامة الصلاة - the establishment of prayer. This means our masajid must come to life on a daily basis. We need to become a culture of Fajr and Isha in congregation, or at the very least, Maghrib and Isha. We must become people of the masjid, people who establish prayer in congregation whether at the masjid or at home.

Then comes zakat as an organized, institutional service. There are now some entrepreneurial efforts by Muslims to create zakat calculators and assistance services, but this needs to be institutionalized even at the masjid level. Someone should be able to walk into a masjid and say, “I need help figuring out my zakat,” and the masjid should have people ready and trained to assist. With modern technology and the internet, we have the opportunity to establish these services nationally and make them easily accessible.

The establishment of commanding good and forbidding evil follows naturally. The more we bring these institutions to life, the better it is for us, because this is what pleases Allah ﷻ. This ayah is describing the very opportunity and responsibility we have been given.

Ayah 42

وَإِن يُكَذِّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَعَادٌ وَثَمُودُ

The tone shifts dramatically. If they reject you and call you a liar, know that the nations of Nuh (AS), Aad, and Thamud all rejected their messengers before them. This is the beginning of a consolation to the Prophet ﷺ, reminding him that he is not alone in facing rejection.

Ayah 43

وَقَوْمُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَقَوْمُ لُوطٍ

The list continues: the people of Ibrahim (AS) and the people of Lut (AS) also rejected their prophets. We are no different from those nations in our capacity to reject truth.

Ayah 44

وَأَصْحَـٰبُ مَدْيَنَ ۖ وَكُذِّبَ مُوسَىٰ فَأَمْلَيْتُ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهُمْ ۖ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ نَكِيرِ

And the people of Madyan rejected their messenger, and even Musa (AS) was rejected and called a liar. Then Allah ﷻ says something chilling: “But I extended My leash on the disbelievers.”

The word أَمْلَيْتُ carries the image of giving someone a long rope, letting them think they have freedom, allowing them to get away with crime after crime, with evil after evil. When your leash is so long, you begin to think you have no leash at all. It’s like using a wireless microphone at a conference - you forget there are any limitations until suddenly you’ve walked too far and pulled out the cable. Allah ﷻ lets people go on and on until they think they are completely free from consequences.

“Then I seized them” - when they least expected it, when they thought they had gotten away with everything, Allah’s grip closed around them. فَكَيْفَ كَانَ نَكِيرِ - “Then how was My punishment?” The word نَكِير refers to something shocking, unexpected, something that comes out of nowhere. It also carries the meaning of something intolerable, something so horrible you cannot imagine being in that situation. How was My punishment that nobody saw coming, that was beyond imagination, that was utterly unbearable?

Ayah 45

فَكَأَيِّن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهَا وَهِىَ ظَالِمَةٌ فَهِىَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا وَبِئْرٍ مُّعَطَّلَةٍ وَقَصْرٍ مَّشِيدٍ

How many towns have there been that We destroyed while they were wronging themselves? These towns now lie خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا - collapsed upon their rooftops. This phrase paints a haunting picture. It doesn’t necessarily mean the town was literally flipped upside down, but rather that the town was destroyed and abandoned. Nobody remained to maintain the buildings. The ceilings collapsed first - that’s always the first thing to go. Then the walls caved in on top of the collapsed ceilings. Centuries of deterioration followed.

وَبِئْرٍ مُّعَطَّلَةٍ - “And wells left unused and unusable.” The word مُعَطَّلَة refers to something rendered completely useless, something that once had function but now serves no purpose. These were wells that once sustained entire communities, now dry and abandoned.

وَقَصْرٍ مَّشِيدٍ - “And castles that were reinforced.” The word مَّشِيدٍ comes from the root meaning to plaster or reinforce with cement. These were fortified castles, built with careful brickwork and plastered for extra strength. Yet all that engineering, all that fortification, meant nothing when Allah’s punishment came. The strongest castles of the wrongdoers now stand as empty ruins.

Ayah 46

أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَتَكُونَ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ يَعْقِلُونَ بِهَآ أَوْ ءَاذَانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَا ۖ فَإِنَّهَا لَا تَعْمَى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ وَلَـٰكِن تَعْمَى ٱلْقُلُوبُ ٱلَّتِى فِى ٱلصُّدُورِ

Haven’t they traveled through the land? This question cuts deep. People have traveled, they have seen ruins, they have passed by the remains of destroyed civilizations. But the question isn’t just about physical travel - it’s about traveling with awareness.

فَتَكُونَ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ يَعْقِلُونَ بِهَآ - “So that they could have hearts they think with.” This is remarkable. We usually think with our minds, but Allah ﷻ speaks of thinking with hearts. What does it mean to think with your heart? It means to think with humility. To think with gratitude. To think with the spiritual awareness that connects physical reality to divine truth. It’s thinking that carries revelation, that carries fear of Allah ﷻ, that carries understanding beyond the material.

أَوْ ءَاذَانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَا - “Or ears they hear with.” Again, not just physical ears. A dog can hear. A bird can hear. Sound waves bounce off any creature’s eardrums. What makes human hearing special? We’re supposed to hear at a level beyond mere creature - we’re supposed to hear in a way that penetrates our hearts, that carries meaning beyond the physical sound.

Then comes the powerful conclusion: فَإِنَّهَا لَا تَعْمَى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ وَلَـٰكِن تَعْمَى ٱلْقُلُوبُ ٱلَّتِى فِى ٱلصُّدُورِ - “For indeed, it is not the eyes that grow blind, but it is the hearts within the chests that grow blind.”

Physical blindness is not the real problem. The real blindness is the inability of the heart to see truth, to recognize divine signs, to understand the lessons written in the ruins they pass by. The hearts inside the chests - that’s where true blindness occurs.

Ayah 47

وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِٱلْعَذَابِ وَلَن يُخْلِفَ ٱللَّهُ وَعْدَهُۥ ۚ وَإِنَّ يَوْمًا عِندَ رَبِّكَ كَأَلْفِ سَنَةٍ مِّمَّا تَعُدُّونَ

They rush you to bring about the punishment - “Come on, where is it? Let’s have it already!” This is the taunt of disbelievers who mock the idea of divine punishment.

Allah’s response is certain: وَلَن يُخْلِفَ ٱللَّهُ وَعْدَهُۥ - “And Allah will never go back on His promise.” The punishment is coming. It will arrive at its appointed time. You’re rushing toward it thinking it won’t come, but it absolutely will. Allah ﷻ does not break His promises.

Then comes a perspective-shifting statement: وَإِنَّ يَوْمًا عِندَ رَبِّكَ كَأَلْفِ سَنَةٍ مِّمَّا تَعُدُّونَ - “And indeed, a day with your Master is like a thousand years of what you count.”

What’s the point of mentioning this in this context? They’re asking for punishment to come quickly. But when that day arrives - whether it’s the Day of Judgment or the day of their destruction - they think it will just be over in a few hours because it’s called “a day.” But a day with Allah ﷻ is like a thousand years from our counting. Do you even realize what you’re asking for? Do you understand the magnitude of what you’re rushing toward? You think it will be brief, but the experience of that day will be incomprehensibly long and severe.

Ayah 48

وَكَأَيِّن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ أَمْلَيْتُ لَهَا وَهِىَ ظَالِمَةٌ ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهَا وَإِلَىَّ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

And how many towns have there been that I gave them more leash, I extended their rope, even though they were doing wrong to themselves. Then I seized them. وَإِلَىَّ ٱلْمَصِيرُ - “And to Me is the final return.”

Everyone will return to Allah ﷻ eventually. The extended leash is temporary. The rope has an end. The return is inevitable.

Ayah 49

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ

The Prophet ﷺ is commanded to make a clear declaration: “Say: O people, I am nothing to you except a giver of very clear warnings.”

This statement tells us something about the tone of what has come before. The discussion of destroyed nations, divine punishment, and extended ropes - all of this is primarily warning, not promise of reward. The Prophet’s role is being clarified: he is a نَذِير - a warner who alerts people to danger ahead.

Ayah 50

فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ

After the warning comes the hope: Those who believed and did righteous deeds will have forgiveness and رِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ - gracious compensation, noble provision.

The word كَرِيم deserves deeper reflection. It comes from كَرَامَة - to give somebody respect, dignity, nobility. What makes compensation “gracious”? Your regular paycheck is not gracious compensation in this sense. The concept is about the manner of giving, the dignity in how provision is offered.

There’s a way to pay your worker that’s insulting - throwing money on the ground and saying “pick it up.” That’s degrading. We don’t give animals gracious compensation; we throw hay on the ground. Even handing someone their earned paycheck can be done in an insulting way, as if you’re doing them a favor rather than giving them what they’ve earned.

Gracious compensation recognizes dignity. It’s like an awards ceremony where people are honored as they receive what they’ve earned. This is how Allah ﷻ will provide for the believers - not just giving them Paradise, but giving it to them in a way that honors and dignifies them, that recognizes their worth and their efforts.

Ayah 51

وَٱلَّذِينَ سَعَوْا۟ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا مُعَـٰجِزِينَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ

In contrast, those who made efforts regarding Our ayat as مُعَـٰجِزِينَ - those are the companions of the Jahim, the roaring flames.

The word مُعَـٰجِزِينَ needs explanation. It comes from the root meaning to compete, to try to overpower, to prove superiority. The classical scholars explain it as trying to compete with someone to see who can overpower the other, who can subdue the other, who can prove once and for all who is best. It means trying to be toe-to-toe with something, to match it and defeat it.

These are people who studied the Quran carefully, who looked at all the ayat, who made tremendous efforts - but for what purpose? To find flaws. To pick apart the verses. To show that their view, their philosophy, their logic is above and beyond the Quran. They wanted to prove the Quran wrong, to demonstrate their superiority over divine revelation.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ - Those are the companions of Jahim. The word الْجَحِيمِ refers to the roaring, raging flames. It captures the intensity and fury of the Fire. These flames will stare at such people with the glare of a hungry lion. That’s the fate of those who spent their intellectual efforts trying to defeat Allah’s words rather than understand and submit to them.

Ayah 52

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ وَلَا نَبِىٍّ إِلَّآ إِذَا تَمَنَّىٰٓ أَلْقَى ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فِىٓ أُمْنِيَّتِهِۦ فَيَنسَخُ ٱللَّهُ مَا يُلْقِى ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ ثُمَّ يُحْكِمُ ٱللَّهُ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

This ayah requires careful understanding. First, notice that Allah ﷻ mentions both رَسُول (messenger) and نَبِىّ (prophet) separately. When these two terms come together in the Quran, it highlights that there is a distinction between them. This tells us that not all prophets were messengers, though all messengers were prophets.

The ayah continues: “Except that when he wished…” What does a prophet wish for? If you told a prophet, “You can have anything you want,” what would he ask for? Not wealth, not power, not comfort. He would wish for his people to become believers. That’s the deepest wish of every prophet’s heart: “If my people would just believe, my mission would be complete. I don’t want Allah ﷻ to punish them. These are my people. If their hearts could just soften to Islam, I would be so happy.”

A prophet sees certain influential people in society and thinks, “If they became Muslim, it would be transformative.” That’s why the Prophet ﷺ made dua for Umar (RA) - he was an influential, respected, powerful figure. If he became Muslim, people would be interested in Islam. He would bring strength to the Muslim community and spark curiosity about Islam among others. The prophets have such wishes - specific people they hope will embrace the truth.

But then أَلْقَى ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فِىٓ أُمْنِيَّتِهِۦ - Shaytan throws something into his wishes. Shaytan disrupts those plans. He throws a wrench into the works. This is not about Shaytan making waswasah (whispers) to the prophet himself - that’s a misunderstanding of the ayah. Rather, it’s about Shaytan disrupting what the prophet wishes for.

Allah ﷻ explains elsewhere: “We made for every prophet enemies from among human beings and jinn” (6:112). These enemies work to dismantle the prophet’s efforts. That person who was going to come to Islam doesn’t come anymore. Or worse, they become the worst enemy of Islam. That individual the prophet had real hopes for - Shaytan ensures they remain in disbelief or even become actively hostile.

Consider examples from history: Nuh (AS) had a profound wish that his son would become a believer. Shaytan threw a wrench into that wish, whispering to the son, leading him to his destruction. The son of a prophet died as a disbeliever.

The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ desperately wished for his uncle Abu Talib to accept Islam. Abu Talib was on the verge of accepting. But who threw a wrench at the very end? Shaytan sent some women from Quraysh to speak to him on his deathbed. Abu Talib’s resolve weakened. He couldn’t hold on. He died without accepting Islam, despite being so close.

These disruptions cause immense pain to the prophets. But then: فَيَنسَخُ ٱللَّهُ مَا يُلْقِى ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ - Allah cancels out what Shaytan throws. Notice: Allah ﷻ doesn’t prevent Shaytan from disrupting the prophet’s wishes. The disruption happens. The painful event occurs. But Allah ﷻ cancels out its negative effects and consequences.

He removes the depression the prophet would have carried. He eliminates the sadness that would have weighed on his heart. He ensures the prophet’s work is not ultimately hampered by these setbacks. The damage Shaytan tried to cause is undone in its impact.

ثُمَّ يُحْكِمُ ٱللَّهُ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ - Then Allah fortifies His ayat, makes them strong and unshakeable. Despite Shaytan’s attempts to disrupt the prophets’ work, Allah’s revelations remain firm and His divine plan continues.

وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ - And Allah is Knowing and Wise. He knows exactly what Shaytan is doing, and His wisdom encompasses the purpose behind allowing these tests and then resolving them.

Ayah 53

لِّيَجْعَلَ مَا يُلْقِى ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فِتْنَةً لِّلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ وَٱلْقَاسِيَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَفِى شِقَاقٍۭ بَعِيدٍ

This ayah reveals a profound reality: whatever problems Shaytan creates, whatever disruptions he causes to the prophets’ wishes, become a severe test - فِتْنَة - for those who already have disease in their hearts.

Here’s how it works: Shaytan creates a problem that hurts the prophet. The prophet recovers from it through Allah’s support. But people who have مَرَض - disease - in their hearts look at that same problem and use it as an excuse not to come to Islam.

They say things like: “What kind of prophet is this? His own uncle couldn’t even become Muslim!” Or: “His own son rejected his message - how can we trust him?” Or: “Look at the problems and setbacks - if this were really from God, wouldn’t it be easier?”

This is the tragic irony: Shaytan first brings the problem to hurt the prophet. The prophet, with Allah’s help, overcomes it. But then those with diseased hearts use that very same problem as justification for their disbelief. The test that was meant to harm the prophet becomes an excuse for the hard-hearted to reject truth.

وَٱلْقَاسِيَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ - “And those whose hearts are hardened.” How hard their hearts have become that they can witness all the signs, see all the evidences, yet still find excuses to turn away.

وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَفِى شِقَاقٍۭ بَعِيدٍ - “And indeed, the wrongdoers are in extreme opposition.” The word شِقَاق means splitting, division, being in opposition. These wrongdoers are in بَعِيد - distant, far, extreme - opposition. They are deeply entrenched in their resistance to truth. No matter what signs they see, no matter what evidences are presented, they remain stubbornly opposed, finding excuse after excuse to justify their rejection of the message.

This is the devastating consequence of having disease and hardness in the heart: the same events that strengthen believers become barriers for the hard-hearted. What should draw them closer to truth pushes them further away. Their opposition becomes more entrenched, more distant, more impossible to bridge.

Ayah 54–72 · linked video: 08. Al-Haj (Ayah 54-72)

Ayah 54

وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ فَيُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَتُخْبِتَ لَهُۥ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهَادِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ

This ayah teaches us that those who have been granted true knowledge will recognize revelation as the absolute truth from their Master. When you witness the theories Allah ﷻ presents in the Quran manifest before your eyes in real life, you should recognize that this is exactly what Allah ﷻ spoke about. The ayah describes how Shaytan comes and creates problems, and other people use those very problems to justify their rejection of faith. They say, “See? This is why we shouldn’t be Muslim, or at least not take it so seriously.” When you observe this pattern unfolding before your eyes, if you have been given knowledge, you recognize it immediately. You say to yourself, “SubhanAllah, Allah ﷻ spoke the truth. This is exactly what He said would happen, and now it’s happening right in front of me.”

Those who have been given knowledge will come to know that this revelation is the truth from their Master. فَيُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهِۦ - then they will believe in it. فَتُخْبِتَ لَهُۥ قُلُوبُهُمْ - As a result, their hearts will become soft and mellowed before Allah ﷻ. The word إِخْبَات conveys a heart that has been humbled and softened, a heart that has surrendered peacefully.

This is a remarkable feature of the Quran. Allah ﷻ speaks about certain concepts in theory, certain psychological and emotional states, certain social patterns. Then, when you see these very concepts manifest in your life, in your family, among your friends, you’re struck with awe. You realize that your Master knows this reality better than you ever imagined. You would never see the correlation between what Allah ﷻ knows, what revelation tells us, and what’s actually happening around us until you become a serious student of the Quran. Then you start seeing these patterns manifest all around you, and it genuinely mellows a person down. You find yourself thinking, “This cannot be the word of a human being.”

The ayah concludes: وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهَادِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ - Allah ﷻ is absolutely determined to guide those who truly believe to a straight path. This comes immediately after the discussion of Shaytan, and someone might start thinking that Shaytan’s influence is so strong. But Allah ﷻ reassures us: those who are meant to come to Iman will absolutely find their way. Allah ﷻ will guide them to the straight path.

Ayah 55

وَلَا يَزَالُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّنْهُ حَتَّىٰ تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمْ عَذَابُ يَوْمٍ عَقِيمٍ

The disbelievers, on the other hand, continue to be in مِرْيَة - doubt about something that isn’t doubtful at all. They harbor doubt about something that shouldn’t be doubted. It’s as if someone said, “I’m not so sure if the sun is warm.” The response would be, “What’s wrong with you? It’s obvious!” Yet Allah ﷻ tells us that the truth of Islam is even more obvious than the sun being warm, but these people still fall into doubt about something that has become so clear and manifest to them.

This state of doubt will persist حَتَّىٰ تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً - until the Hour arrives at them suddenly, without expectation, without even imagination. They couldn’t even imagine this kind of reckoning. أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمْ عَذَابُ يَوْمٍ عَقِيمٍ - or the punishment of a يَوْم عَقِيم comes to them.

The word عَقِيم is significant. It refers to land that doesn’t produce any vegetation, land that is barren. It’s also used for a man or a woman who cannot have children - they are childless, infertile. A wind that produces no good is called رِيح عَقِيم - a wind that doesn’t bring clouds, that doesn’t bring rain to pollinate the land, that brings no good news but only brings disease or harm. So يَوْم عَقِيم is a day that has no goodness in it whatsoever. There is no relief in it, no mercy, no respite.

Ayah 56

ٱلْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلَّهِ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فِى جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ

The kingdom on that Day belongs entirely to Allah ﷻ. He is the One who judges between them. Then those who believed and acted righteously will be in جَنَّات النَّعِيم - gardens that can only be characterized as permanent blessings, permanent luxury.

Notice the contrast Allah ﷻ establishes. On one hand, there is permanent restlessness and barrenness - يَوْم عَقِيم. On the other side, there is permanent luxury and blessing - نَعِيم. One group faces a day with no relief, while the other enjoys blessings that never end.

Ayah 57

وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ

As for those who disbelieved and lied against Our miraculous signs, they are the ones who will have عَذَاب مُهِين - humiliating punishment. The punishment is not just painful; it is specifically characterized as humiliating, stripping away any remaining dignity.

Ayah 58

وَٱلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ قُتِلُوٓا۟ أَوْ مَاتُوا۟ لَيَرْزُقَنَّهُمُ ٱللَّهُ رِزْقًا حَسَنًا ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ

Allah ﷻ now speaks about those who migrated in His path. Notice that the Surah isn’t just speaking about the hijrah that has already occurred; rather, it’s addressing the Sahaba (RA) about what’s coming. Those who migrated in Allah’s path ثُمَّ قُتِلُوٓا۟ - then they were killed.

This is significant because most of the Sahaba (RA) weren’t killed when they made hijrah. So what is this ayah discussing? It’s addressing the next phase after hijrah - the phase of fighting that is coming. أَوْ مَاتُوا۟ - or they simply died, whether they died on the way or died peacefully in Madinah.

لَيَرْزُقَنَّهُمُ ٱللَّهُ رِزْقًا حَسَنًا - Allah ﷻ absolutely guarantees that He will provide them, He will compensate them with a beautiful compensation. وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ - And no doubt, Allah ﷻ truly is the best of those who can provide.

Think about what these people left behind. They left their businesses, and businesses are like children to business owners. Business owners nurture them, care for them every single day. They left their homes - and building a home is not easy. It takes years of effort and maintenance. They left their extended families, their sources of income, their entire means of provision. For people to leave all of this behind requires extraordinary sacrifice.

Allah ﷻ says: I will absolutely compensate you for the kind of sacrifice you made for Me. This is actually a continuation of what we read earlier in the Surah: لَيَنصُرَنَّ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَنصُرُهُۥ - Allah ﷻ will absolutely aid the one who aided Him. Why did these people leave their homes? Because they recognized that their personal comfort, their business, their home - none of it was as important as the mission of the Messenger ﷺ that they had just accepted.

It wasn’t enough for them to simply become Muslim and believe in him from a distance. They could have said, “If he goes to Madinah, so be it. I can believe in him long-distance. It’s all good.” But they didn’t. They said, “I am with him now. I am with him in this mission.” Their closeness to Allah ﷻ became defined by who was with the Prophet ﷺ and his mission.

Ayah 59

لَيُدْخِلَنَّهُم مُّدْخَلًا يَرْضَوْنَهُۥ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلِيمٌ حَلِيمٌ

Allah ﷻ will absolutely enter them into a مُدْخَل - a place of entrance. This is a remarkable expression. The gate itself is going to be so grand, so magnificent, that people will look at the gate of Jannah and say, “Whoa! Can we just stay here? Is this my reward?” But that’s just the entrance.

يَرْضَوْنَهُۥ - The gates themselves are going to please them. They’re going to be so delighted with what they see at the entrance that they’ll be overwhelmed. Some scholars say this phrase means they will be pleased with Allah ﷻ when they see those gates, recognizing His generosity and mercy in what He has prepared for them.

وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلِيمٌ حَلِيمٌ - Absolutely, Allah ﷻ, no doubt about it, truly does know everything, and He is حَلِيم - forbearing. He knows what you’re going through. He sympathizes with you. He understands your struggles.

Ayah 60

۞ ذَٰلِكَ وَمَنْ عَاقَبَ بِمِثْلِ مَا عُوقِبَ بِهِۦ ثُمَّ بُغِىَ عَلَيْهِ لَيَنصُرَنَّهُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ

ذَٰلِكَ - You get that? Did that get through to you? This word appears multiple times in this Surah. It’s part of the stylistic pattern of Surah Al-Hajj - emphasizing points repeatedly.

وَمَنْ عَاقَبَ بِمِثْلِ مَا عُوقِبَ بِهِۦ - Whoever retaliates in equal measure to how he was retaliated against. The word عَاقَبَ means to follow up something with something back that’s equal. ثُمَّ بُغِىَ عَلَيْهِ - Then he is rebelled against again, then wrong is done to him once more.

What does this mean? The Arabs of Makkah attacked the Muslims because they believed they were justified in retaliating against what they perceived as the offensive message of Islam. They thought they had legitimate grievances. Now, finally at Badr, the Muslims would have their chance to respond to years of persecution. But Allah ﷻ is alluding to the fact that even after the Muslim retaliation at Badr, the disbelievers are going to come back and wrong them again.

لَيَنصُرَنَّهُ ٱللَّهُ - Such a person, Allah ﷻ will absolutely aid. Notice what Allah ﷻ could have said. He could have simply stated, “They will attack you and attack you again,” leaving the believers in fear and worry. Instead, Allah ﷻ says: whoever experiences this pattern of persecution and retaliation should know one thing - they don’t need any armies. All they need to know is that Allah ﷻ will absolutely aid such a person.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ - No doubt Allah ﷻ is عَفُوّ - extremely, lovingly forgiving, and غَفُور - He covers all kinds of mistakes and does it continuously. Allah ﷻ mentions His forgiveness at the end of this ayah perhaps alluding to the fact that those who fight in the path of Allah ﷻ are running toward forgiveness. They’re seeking Allah’s forgiveness through their sacrifice.

Ayah 61

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَيُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ

That is because Allah ﷻ makes the night penetrate into the day, and Allah ﷻ makes the day penetrate into the night. What does this have to do with fighting the disbelievers?

The answer: seasons change, days change. The day of Quraysh’s dominance is over, and their night is about to begin. But just because their night began and your day started doesn’t mean your day will last forever. Days turn into nights and nights turn into days. There’s a cycle to all things. Don’t think that because you’re experiencing victory today that it will continue unchanged, and don’t think that because you’re experiencing defeat today that it’s permanent.

وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ - And Allah ﷻ is all-hearing and all-seeing. He hears everything and sees everything that’s happening in these cycles of day and night, victory and defeat.

Ayah 62

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَأَنَّ مَا يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ هُوَ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْكَبِيرُ

That is because Allah ﷻ - He Himself is الْحَقّ - the ultimate Truth. وَأَنَّ مَا يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ هُوَ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ - And everything that they’re calling upon besides Him, all of it is بَاطِل - false, void, meaningless.

وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْكَبِيرُ - And Allah ﷻ - He Himself is the ultimately High, the Great. These Names affirm Allah’s absolute transcendence and majesty above all of creation.

Ayah 63

أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَتُصْبِحُ ٱلْأَرْضُ مُخْضَرَّةً ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَطِيفٌ خَبِيرٌ

Didn’t you ever observe how Allah ﷻ sent water down from the sky, and then the earth turns مُخْضَرَّة - green? إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَطِيفٌ خَبِيرٌ - Certainly Allah ﷻ is لَطِيف - very subtle, and He is خَبِير - He knows fully.

This ayah about bringing life out of death comes immediately after the discussion of fighting. When fighting is mentioned repeatedly, the reality of death becomes prominent. You might die, there’s a strong possibility you’re going to die. So Allah ﷻ says: Don’t worry about it. I raise you again. I raise plants all the time. This is the connection - just as dead earth comes to life with rain, so too will you be brought back to life. Death is not the end.

Ayah 64

لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ

He exclusively owns whatever is in the skies and whatever is in the earth. وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ - And it is certainly Allah ﷻ, truly He is الْغَنِىّ - the One who is free of all needs, completely independent, doesn’t need anybody, not even to praise Him.

الْحَمِيد is beautiful. It comes from the same root as حَمِد and مَحْمُود and حَامِد. He is always praised. Never does His praise stop, and He doesn’t need anybody to do it either. He is praised in and of Himself. If nothing existed, He would still be praised. His praiseworthiness is intrinsic to His essence.

Ayah 65

أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَخَّرَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلْفُلْكَ تَجْرِى فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِۦ وَيُمْسِكُ ٱلسَّمَآءَ أَن تَقَعَ عَلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِٱلنَّاسِ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ

Didn’t you notice how Allah ﷻ subdued and submitted for you whatever is on the earth? And on top of that, the large vessels - الْفُلْك - that flow in the ocean by His command?

وَيُمْسِكُ ٱلسَّمَآءَ أَن تَقَعَ عَلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ - And He holds the sky back that it should fall upon the earth. يُمْسِك means He holds it back, He holds it up. Someone might object: “This sounds unscientific. The sky is just gas or vacuum, so what does it mean that He’s holding it up?”

The response is this: is the only thing above us gas and vacuum? Are there not other planets? Are there not meteors and stars? Is there not an almost infinite number of objects, many of them much larger than the earth, floating about in the universe at insane speeds? Allah ﷻ says السَّمَاء - and by the way, this word doesn’t just mean “sky” in the limited sense. It means whatever is above you. Allah ﷻ is holding it back from smashing into this planet and ending its story.

How many explosions happen in the universe that astronomers observe through their powerful telescopes? How many galaxies are destroyed? How many black holes consume entire systems? Yet all of that chaos hasn’t reached us. Who keeps it all back? Some will tell you we’re just a product of chance. “One day a meteor is going to come and it’s going to be over,” they’ll say. “Or one day the sun is going to run out of fuel and that will be it.” They present these scenarios as if they’re governed by random chance.

But Allah ﷻ says: I’m the One holding it in place. إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦٓ - except by His permission. Nothing falls except when He permits it.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِٱلنَّاسِ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ - No doubt, Allah ﷻ, especially when it comes to people, is extremely compassionate, extremely empathizing, and always merciful.

Ayah 66

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَحْيَاكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَكَفُورٌ

He is the One who gave you life, ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ - then He will give you death, ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ - then He will bring you back to life again.

This parallels themes we find in Surah Al-Baqarah. The Quran often creates these beautiful connections between surahs. Here Allah ﷻ is saying: I gave you life out of nothing, out of non-existence. I will give you death. Then I will bring you back to life again and give you the opportunity for eternal life.

إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَكَفُورٌ - The human being is so extremely ungrateful. After all of this - life, death, resurrection, and the promise of eternal life - how can you be ungrateful?

Ayah 67

لِّكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ جَعَلْنَا مَنسَكًا هُمْ نَاسِكُوهُ ۖ فَلَا يُنَـٰزِعُنَّكَ فِى ٱلْأَمْرِ ۚ وَٱدْعُ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ ۖ إِنَّكَ لَعَلَىٰ هُدًى مُّسْتَقِيمٍ

For every nation We have set a مَنسَك - a place of sacrifice, a tradition, a time for ritual sacrifice. هُمْ نَاسِكُوهُ - they engage in that sacrifice the way they are told.

Notice that hajj has been mentioned twice in this Surah. This is part of the literary style of Surah Al-Hajj - main subjects are revisited. Every few ayahs, the same theme comes back again. It’s a deliberate structural feature.

فَلَا يُنَـٰزِعُنَّكَ فِى ٱلْأَمْرِ - Then they shouldn’t نَزَع you in the matter. نَزَع means to disagree, to argue, to pull back and forth, to snatch or yank something that was at rest. The imagery is of people pulling one another, debating: “Our way of sacrifice is better. Your way of sacrifice is wrong.” They’re arguing and debating about these ritual matters.

Allah ﷻ says everyone was given their own way. They don’t have to debate this with you and target you for following your tradition. وَٱدْعُ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ - Just call on your Master. Don’t get entangled in these debates. إِنَّكَ لَعَلَىٰ هُدًى مُّسْتَقِيمٍ - You are, no doubt, committed to straight guidance. In other words, O Prophet ﷺ, when these kinds of arguments come, leave them. Your time is better spent making dua.

Ayah 68

وَإِن جَـٰدَلُوكَ فَقُلِ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ

And if they still want to come and جَـٰدَل - debate with you, فَقُلِ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ - then tell them: Allah ﷻ knows better about the things that you’re doing.

تَعْمَلُونَ is a very powerful word in Arabic. As opposed to فِعْل, which primarily indicates action, عَمَل includes both action and intention. So what the Prophet ﷺ is telling them is: Allah ﷻ doesn’t just know the sacrifices you perform. He knows your motivations behind them too. He knows what’s in your hearts when you do these acts of worship.

Ayah 69

ٱللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ

As we approach the conclusion of the Surah, notice that the beginning of the Surah discussed people who debate, and now the end of the Surah is also addressing people who debate. This creates a beautiful frame for the entire Surah.

ٱللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ - Allah ﷻ, in fact, is going to judge between you on the Day of Resurrection. فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ - In all the things that you used to disagree with each other about, Allah ﷻ will settle those disagreements with ultimate justice.

Ayah 70

أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ فِى كِتَـٰبٍ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ

Didn’t you already know that Allah ﷻ knows, in fact, everything in the sky - everything that’s above - and everything on the earth? إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ فِى كِتَـٰبٍ - All of that is declared in a book, in a record. إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ - That is very easy for Allah ﷻ. That, no doubt, is easy only for Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 71

وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ سُلْطَـٰنًا وَمَا لَيْسَ لَهُم بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۗ وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِن نَّصِيرٍ

And still they worship other than Allah ﷻ. Despite knowing what you know, despite appreciating what Allah ﷻ knows and His comprehensive knowledge, they still choose to worship others besides Him.

مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ سُلْطَـٰنًا - They worship entities about whom Allah ﷻ did not send any سُلْطَان - any authority, any validation. These false gods have no legitimacy whatsoever. وَمَا لَيْسَ لَهُم بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ - And they themselves have no knowledge of these deities on their own either. They have no rational basis, no revealed basis, nothing.

وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِن نَّصِيرٍ - And for those kinds of wrongdoers, there’s not going to be any helper whatsoever. No one will come to their aid.

This ayah is actually a continuation of themes from earlier in the Surah. Previously, we learned how people fabricate their mythologies about their false gods. Now Allah ﷻ concludes: they have no knowledge of it whatsoever. They’re making it all up.

Ayah 72

وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ تَعْرِفُ فِى وُجُوهِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلْمُنكَرَ ۖ يَكَادُونَ يَسْطُونَ بِٱلَّذِينَ يَتْلُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا ۗ قُلْ أَفَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِشَرٍّ مِّن ذَٰلِكُمُ ۗ ٱلنَّارُ وَعَدَهَا ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

When you recite Our clear, miraculous signs to them, تَعْرِفُ فِى وُجُوهِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلْمُنكَرَ - you’re going to recognize in the faces of those who disbelieve الْمُنكَر - denial, a look of rejection. They’ll make this weird face as if what they’re hearing is completely unheard of, completely objectionable. “What? That’s supposed to be from God?” You’ll recognize that expression, that face they’re making.

الْمُنكَر also means evil. You’ll see an evil look on their faces, a look of hatred and rejection.

يَكَادُونَ يَسْطُونَ بِٱلَّذِينَ يَتْلُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا - They will almost سَطَا - try to grab, attack those who are reading the Quran to them. When the Quran is being recited, they get so angry that the Shaytan inside them starts going crazy. They get enraged. They almost physically attack the people who are reciting Quran. They can’t stand hearing it recited. سَطَا means to grab forcefully, to attack somebody violently.

قُلْ أَفَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِشَرٍّ مِّن ذَٰلِكُمُ - Tell them: You think the Quran is so offensive that you want to get rid of it? You don’t want to hear it at all? Can I tell you of something far worse than that for you people?

ٱلنَّارُ - The Fire! وَعَدَهَا ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ - Allah ﷻ has promised it to those who disbelieved. وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ - And what a horrible final destination that is.

Ayah 73 · linked video: 09. Al-Haj (Ayah 73)

Surah Al-Hajj, Ayah 73

Ayah 73

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ضُرِبَ مَثَلٌ فَٱسْتَمِعُوا۟ لَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ لَن يَخْلُقُوا۟ ذُبَابًا وَلَوِ ٱجْتَمَعُوا۟ لَهُۥ ۖ وَإِن يَسْلُبْهُمُ ٱلذُّبَابُ شَيْـًٔا لَّا يَسْتَنقِذُوهُ مِنْهُ ۚ ضَعُفَ ٱلطَّالِبُ وَٱلْمَطْلُوبُ

O humanity! An example has been struck, so listen to it carefully. Indeed, those you invoke besides Allah will never create a fly, even if they all came together for that purpose. And if the fly should snatch something away from them, they could not retrieve it from the fly. Weak are the seeker and the sought.

This passage represents some of the most powerful imagery in the Quran, and it concludes Surah Al-Hajj with a message that every human being needs to internalize. Notice how the surah began with “O humanity” (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ), warning of the shaking of the Hour. Now it ends with the same address, but this time with guidance that applies immediately to our lives, not just to some distant cosmic event.

The Psychology Behind the Opening

Allah ﷻ says ضُرِبَ مَثَلٌ (an example has been struck), using the passive voice. He doesn’t say “Allah gave an example” or “I am giving you an example.” This is deliberate and profound. Throughout this surah, Allah ﷻ has described Himself extensively—His knowledge, His power, His control over all affairs. But by this point, it seems the disbelievers have reached a stage where they’ve tuned out simply because they know the message comes from Allah ﷻ. They’ve stopped listening to the content because they’ve rejected the source.

This is what social psychologists call “peripheral route persuasion.” People don’t just evaluate information on its merits—they judge it based on who’s saying it, how they’re dressed, their accent, their background. Most people are more influenced by these peripheral factors than by the actual evidence presented. The moment they see who’s speaking or where the message comes from, they shut down. “Oh, it’s from him? I’m not listening.”

This isn’t unique to the disbelievers of Makkah. We Muslims do this constantly. Imagine a young man shows up early for Jumu’ah prayer, wearing a baseball cap sideways, with an elaborate beard and baggy clothes. He makes his way to the front and approaches the minbar. What’s the reaction? People check their watches. The elders wonder if there’s time to make it to another masjid. Even if this young man delivers the most profound khutbah they’ve ever heard, many won’t be able to receive it because of how he looks. Meanwhile, those who came late and only heard a snippet might walk away inspired, precisely because they didn’t see him first.

So Allah ﷻ removes Himself from the equation here. He uses the passive voice: “An example has been struck.” Don’t focus on who’s giving the example—focus on the example itself. Then He says فَٱسْتَمِعُوا۟ لَهُۥٓ (listen to it), not “listen to Me.” Listen to the argument, not to the speaker.

For believers, the opposite applies. When we know something comes from Allah ﷻ, we pay extra attention. That’s why elsewhere in the Quran, when addressing believers, Allah ﷻ explicitly mentions Himself: “This is how Allah gives examples to people.” He mentions His name because it increases the believers’ attentiveness.

The Force of “Struck”

The word ضُرِبَ (struck) itself carries impact. In English, we’ve developed our own Muslim vocabulary—we say things like “glad tidings” or “he is upon the truth,” expressions nobody uses outside our community. Similarly, we’ve adopted phrases like “an example has been struck” from Quranic translations. But normally in English, we’d say “gave an example” or “offered an example.”

The Arabic says “struck” because when something strikes something else, it captures attention. It startles you. These aren’t just examples—they’re striking examples, meant to jolt you awake. Allah ﷻ doesn’t say “an example has been offered for teaching purposes.” He says it’s been struck down, demanding your immediate focus.

Who Are Those Called Upon Besides Allah?

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ—“Those you call upon other than Allah.” Who are they? The obvious answer is idols, and yes, that’s true. But it’s much broader than stone statues. This includes anyone who claims divinity, any tyrant ruler who demands worship, any Pharaoh who declares himself god.

In the intellectual age we live in, paganism hasn’t disappeared—it’s evolved. Modern idols are philosophies and ideologies. You take an idea and elevate it to the status of ultimate truth, placing nothing higher than it. The idea itself might not be inherently wrong, but when it becomes your highest truth, your unmoved mover, it functions as your deity.

Someone might say, “The ultimate truth for me is humanitarianism,” or “democracy,” or “science.” These become their Allah (their deity), the one they call upon, the truth that never moves. Allah ﷻ is الحق (The Ultimate Truth), but when you replace Him with any other ultimate truth, you’ve engaged in a form of shirk.

The Devastating Example

لَن يَخْلُقُوا۟ ذُبَابًا وَلَوِ ٱجْتَمَعُوا۟ لَهُۥ—“They will never create a fly, even if they all came together for it.” An army of hundreds of thousands, powerful rulers who command nations, mighty idols before whom millions prostrate—none of them can create a single fly. Earlier in the previous surah, we learned from the story of Ibrahim (AS) that these false gods cannot even defend themselves. Now we learn they can’t even unite to create the smallest, most insignificant creature.

Why a fly specifically? Consider the scene in idol-worshiping temples. Massive statues stand in ornate buildings. Devotees bring offerings—flowers, milk, honey, food—and place them before the idol with incense and elaborate rituals. But when you leave food in the open, what shows up? Flies.

Picture this: A fly lands right on top of the milk offering, directly in front of the idol. It makes eye contact with the statue and casually sits on its nose. Then it flies down and drinks the milk. What are you going to do about it? The worshipers frantically try to shoo it away, but this very act should shatter their faith. Your god cannot defend its own sacred temple from a fly.

If a tourist wandered in and tried to take the chocolate milk, the worshipers would be outraged: “You cannot touch the sacred offering!” But when the fly brazenly steals from their god before their eyes, they’re helpless. The fly destroys the foundations of their faith in real time. There’s a classical Arabic expression: “Don’t underestimate the small one in battle, for the fly can poke out the eye of the lion.” The fly is that audacious.

The Impossibility of Recovery

وَإِن يَسْلُبْهُمُ ٱلذُّبَابُ شَيْـًٔا لَّا يَسْتَنقِذُوهُ مِنْهُ—“And if the fly should snatch something away from them, they cannot retrieve it from the fly.” The word يَسْلُبْ means to snatch boldly, openly, while someone watches. Like when my daughter makes direct eye contact with me and reaches for the tomatoes on my plate—“I’m about to do this right now, what are you going to do?”

Now imagine Pharaoh in his palace, sitting atop his pyramid, eating his royal meal. A fly lands on his lettuce, takes a piece, and flies away. Even if he commands his entire army: “Capture that fly and bring back my lettuce!”—they cannot do it. Once the fly takes something, it’s gone forever.

The scholar Al-Sha’rawi offered a fascinating scientific insight. He studied flies because Allah ﷻ mentioned them, and he discovered that flies release an acidic saliva the instant they land on food. This saliva wraps around the food and chemically alters it permanently upon contact. So even if you somehow captured the fly, invented a microscope on the spot, placed it under examination, and performed surgery to extract that piece of lettuce—it’s no longer lettuce. It’s been chemically transformed. لَّا يَسْتَنقِذُوهُ مِنْهُ—they cannot rescue it, no matter how desperately they want to. They can’t even attempt it.

This is an incredible demonstration of the weakness of shirk, illustrated through the humblest of creatures.

The Universal Lesson: Weakness Defined

ضَعُفَ ٱلطَّالِبُ وَٱلْمَطْلُوبُ—“Weak are the seeker and the sought.” This phrase appears multiple times in the Quran, including in Surah Al-Hadid, and it carries profound implications. The one who wants something (الطالب) is weak, and the thing wanted (المطلوب) is also weak.

Notice the word used: ضَعُفَ. This doesn’t mean “made weak,” as if something external weakened them. The form indicates inherent weakness—they are weak by nature, in their original default state. The idol is weak. The fly is weak. The food is weak. The one who wants the food back is weak. All of them, inherently weak.

But this final statement isn’t limited to Pharaohs and false gods. It applies to every human being. We all want things. I want a promotion. I want a better job. I want to pay off my house. I want a good education for my children. I want to move to this city. I want to marry this person. I want that car. I want the new gaming console. I want that app. I want, I want, I want.

Allah ﷻ tells us: Everyone who wants something is weak, and whatever they want is also weak. You are weak because of the things you want. You are programmed to be weak through your desires.

Understanding Our Weakness

Let me illustrate this weakness. A man desperately wants to marry a particular woman. He’s obsessed with her, and they’re about to get married. The day before the wedding, she says no, or she passes away. What happens to that man? He breaks apart. He collapses. He can’t think straight. He loses his mind.

We want health and safety for our children. If something happens to them, we can literally lose our minds. That’s how precious they are to our hearts.

I studied abnormal psychology in college, and there’s one case I cannot forget. A man was completing his PhD in psychology at NYU in the 1970s. He typed his 400-page thesis—the only copy he had—and submitted it to the office. They lost it. Five years of work, gone. “We don’t have your thesis.”

He lost his mind. To this day, he wanders around campus saying, “Don’t go to school here. They’ll steal your work.” Every few weeks, they take him to Bellevue Hospital’s psychiatric ward, stabilize him, then release him. He returns to campus, warning everyone: “They’ll steal your work.”

The thing he wanted more than anything else was taken away, and it exposed his weakness. He collapsed as a person. The more attached you become to your wants, the weaker you become. The more you cannot survive without them.

Watch our children five minutes away from their devices. “Where is it? I can’t breathe. Can I have it back, please? I can’t live without it.” They become that weak. They genuinely cannot survive without it.

This is what ضَعُفَ ٱلطَّالِبُ وَٱلْمَطْلُوبُ means. This is the nature of being human. This ayah reveals something profound about the origins of depression, sadness, and rage. Most psychological disorders stem from some form of deprivation—you were deprived of something you desperately wanted, and you cannot move past it.

The more we want of this world, the weaker we become. The more dependent we are on things outside our control, the more vulnerable we are to collapse. This is why the believer places their ultimate want, their ultimate desire, with Allah ﷻ alone—the only One who is not weak, the only One who can never be taken away, the only One whose attachment strengthens rather than weakens.

Ayah 74–77 · linked video: 10. Al-Haj (Ayah 74-77)

PRIMARY_AYAH: 74

Ayah 74

مَا قَدَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَقَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌ

Allah ﷻ declares: “They didn’t appreciate Allah like He deserved to be appreciated. They didn’t recognize His value as His value deserved to be recognized.”

When human beings seek everything except Allah ﷻ, they are fundamentally seeking things that are weak by definition. And when you seek what is weak, what does that make you? Weak. But when you seek the ultimately Strong—Allah ﷻ—what does that make you? Strong. This is the only thing you can seek that can never be taken away from you. Everything else around you will eventually go. But belief in Allah ﷻ won’t.

In the previous Surah, Al-Hijr, the Prophet ﷺ was told: “Don’t worry about what We have given them. We’ve given them a lot of things, but the provision from Allah to you is better.” What is that provision from Allah ﷻ? His Word. His Word won’t go anywhere if it’s in your heart.

This ayah is Allah’s comment about the entire human race. Across billions of people on this earth, Allah ﷻ is saying about all of them: they just didn’t realize who He is like He deserved to be realized. They didn’t understand who they were dealing with.

Then He mentions so beautifully: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَقَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌ—no doubt Allah is ultimately Strong. He is the final, the greatest source of strength. The previous ayahs talked about weakness, and now that weakness is contrasted with Allah’s strength. قَوِىّ represents the ultimate strength, and عَزِيز is the ultimate authority.

Ayah 75

ٱللَّهُ يَصْطَفِى مِنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ رُسُلًا وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ

Humanity didn’t appreciate Allah ﷻ, so Allah ﷻ decided to help them. Along the way, Allah ﷻ chose certain people from among humanity who would truly appreciate Him and teach humanity how to appreciate Him.

اللَّهُ يَصْطَفِى—Allah is the One who entirely on His own, He chooses. You didn’t make that decision; Allah ﷻ did. Allah ﷻ decided to choose on His own accord, meaning a choice made with no biases, no influences, entirely on Allah’s own judgment. He chose from among the angels certain messengers, and from among the people also.

In our case, from among the angels, Jibreel (AS) was chosen. Among the legions of angels, Allah ﷻ selected this messenger and said: “This one will deliver My message. This one is suitable. This one will make a good messenger among the angels.”

And then among all the billions of people who live on this planet, Allah ﷻ decided that Muhammad ﷺ—in that time, in that generation, in that region, born of that father and that mother, at that age—should become a messenger. Allah ﷻ chose that cave, that night, that angel from the sky, those words اقْرَأْ, and that messenger—entirely on His choice.

All for what? According to this passage, messengers were chosen because the vast majority of the population of this earth just don’t appreciate who Allah ﷻ is. They don’t know who He is. They have no realization. They go after weak things on their own, showing their weakness. Allah ﷻ wants them to be strong. Allah ﷻ wants to help humanity out.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ—Allah hears everything, He sees everything. He hears the cries of people. He hears the sadness in their hearts. He sees the troubles they go through. And He knows how iman in Him can bring light to their lives when they’re living in darkness. So He sends—He chooses—these messengers.

Ayah 76

يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۗ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ

يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ—He knows what’s right in front of them. What is right in front of them? The Quran is right in front of them. The message of Islam. Muhammad ﷺ is right in front of them. He knows what’s in front of them.

وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ—and He knows what’s behind them. He knows their history. He knows what kind of ignorant life they were living. And this is not just a comment about the disbelievers. This is a comment about all of humanity. He knows what humanity had before and what humanity has now.

Think of this parable: the Quran is like a fire extinguisher. You have a fire extinguisher in your house. Everything around you is burning, and you’re sitting on it, looking at it, thinking: “Oh, this is so beautiful. Let me listen to its sound—it makes such a cool sound.
