Oh, I don’t want anybody touching it. Let me put this fire extinguisher up there.” But what’s the purpose of the fire extinguisher? The fire is getting closer! You were given this to help yourself.

He knows what’s in front of them and what’s behind them. This also means He knows what they’re doing right now, and He knows what they were doing yesterday. On the one hand, you didn’t appreciate Allah ﷻ and you didn’t love Him as you should have. But on the other hand, you didn’t realize that Allah ﷻ knows all of these things, and He never forgets. He keeps a record.

وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ—and the record goes back for processing. To make this easy to understand: you cut a red light and you see a flash, and you know what’s coming. Why? Because that picture is sent for processing to the Department of Motor Vehicles, which is then going to send your high-definition photo to your house with a $500 fine. You know that violation was sent back for processing and finally, prosecution.

Every single decision we make is caught, recorded, and sent back to Allah ﷻ for processing. This is what it means that matters are returned to Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 77

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱرْكَعُوا۟ وَٱسْجُدُوا۟ وَٱعْبُدُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ وَٱفْعَلُوا۟ ٱلْخَيْرَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ ۩

This was humanity’s case. Humanity overwhelmingly has disappointed Allah ﷻ. Humanity didn’t realize who Allah ﷻ is. But Allah ﷻ chose messengers, and these messengers succeeded in at least getting some people to appreciate Allah ﷻ. Those few people who appreciate Allah ﷻ are called believers.

So the next ayah says—not to all of humanity now, because all of humanity is already a disappointment—يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟: “Your turn, believers. Those of you who truly believe, you—the chosen few who appreciate Allah ﷻ—now let Me teach you how to appreciate Me. The rest of humanity isn’t even willing to appreciate Me. At least you have shown the willingness by accepting iman. But even though you have accepted iman, you may not know what to do. So let Me tell you what to do. This is how you appreciate Me. This is how you become strong.”

The entire passage from beginning to end is about becoming strong. And now we’re going to learn how.

ٱرْكَعُوا۟—“Put yourselves in ruku.” We accept this as referring to Salah. Ruku is humility, and ruku is also exhaustion. You see sprinters when they’re tired—what do they do? They literally make ruku. They’re just done: “I am exhausted before You, Allah ﷻ. I don’t know where else to go. I’m giving myself up before You.”

وَٱسْجُدُوا۟—“Then prostrate.” Sometimes when a human being is so exhausted, what happens? He collapses. And if you really lost consciousness, your face would hit the ground, because naturally human beings protect their face. What do we put on the ground when we make sujud? Our face. Our pride.

Learn to come to Allah ﷻ in humility, and then progress even further in your humility. The imagery of Salah itself is one of us becoming more and more humble to Allah ﷻ and engaging in that process continually. Stand up again humbly, then lower yourself a little, then lower yourself more, then stand up again, then lower yourself a little, and lower yourself more.

وَٱعْبُدُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ—“And as a means of that, enslave yourselves to your Master.” If you translate this as “worship your master,” something is missing, because ruku and sujud are already worship. Allah ﷻ is saying: those two acts are going to remind you that you are a slave even outside of those acts. So now those acts will help develop a state of mind in you of being a slave. You will realize that you’re not free. You’re not just free to do whatever you want. You’re not just a seeker, a consumer. You are a slave. You don’t have a free life. You have a restricted life.

From a psychological and spiritual point of view, the people who have the most peace in their lives, who enjoy the most emotional freedom, are the people who accept that they’re slaves of Allah ﷻ. You have to accept slavery to enjoy freedom. And the people who think they’re free? They are slaves to everything. They’re slaves to their temptations, slaves to their appetites, slaves to consumer products, slaves to advertising, slaves to societal trends, slaves to the music industry, the movie industry. Everything commands them and they obey. And the tragedy of all this is they think they’re free.

Consider the modern environment. People think they can dress however they want, do whatever they want. It looks like freedom. But look closer: all the kids in one group dress exactly the same. All the kids in another group dress exactly the same. One group is enslaved to a particular genre of music, another to something else. And if their “uniform” requires them to pierce their nose and eyebrow, wear black lipstick, and color their hair purple, they will do it because it’s the uniform. They have to fit in. And they call themselves different. They’re enslaved to a particular image, and they think it’s freedom.

Are you free enough to dress completely differently tomorrow? “Nope. Can’t do it. My friends will kill me.” That’s not freedom.

Now compare that to a slave of Allah ﷻ. Yes, we have restrictions from Allah ﷻ. But I don’t feel obligated to look like this one or that one or the other. I can look like myself, so long as I’m within the confines of what Allah ﷻ has put on me. I can like what I like and dislike what I dislike. I don’t have to be pressured by society. I’m freed from that. I’m free from all those expectations. This is real freedom.

So engage in slavery to Allah ﷻ. And when you truly become slaves of Allah ﷻ and find yourself free from all else, then you will realize your potential.

وَٱفْعَلُوا۟ ٱلْخَيْرَ—“Then do the ultimate good.” Do good for humanity. Do good for others. Up until now in this ayah, it was all between you and Allah ﷻ. You make ruku, you make sujud, you enslave yourself to your Master. That was all between you and Allah ﷻ. When you really become a slave to the Master and you’re not selfish anymore, when you just want to serve Allah ﷻ, you realize that one of the great ways of serving Allah ﷻ—besides becoming a slave—is that a natural consequence of that is you want to spread that goodness to others. You want to do good to others. You start worrying about others.

As-Salah is for you. But al-khayr is for everybody else. You start getting concerned with others—be it your family, your neighbor. It doesn’t have to be the whole world, but at least the good spreads beyond you. It becomes contagious now.

لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ—“So that all of you may attain success.” Allah ﷻ just talked about failures: people who didn’t appreciate Allah ﷻ, people who are weak, people whose gods can’t even create a fly. Failure, failure, failure. And now Allah ﷻ says: “So that you can attain success.”

Even the word used for success is profound. Allah ﷻ says لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ—so that you may reap the harvest. Success is one thing—a successful businessman, a successful investor, a successful student. But الفلاح is someone who put in a lot of work on his farm the whole year, exhausted himself, and at the end of it all, eventually saw the fruits of his labor in the harvest. That’s called falah.

This word is actually going to trigger the first passage of the next Surah: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ—“The true believers have already attained true, real success.” This conversation is going to flow into the next Surah. But for now, we’re still on the believers.

Ayah 78 · linked video: 11. Al-Haj (Ayah 78)

Surah Al-Hajj: Ayah 78

وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ فِى ٱللَّهِ حَقَّ جِهَادِهِۦ ۚ هُوَ ٱجْتَبَىٰكُمْ وَمَا جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ ۚ مِّلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ۚ هُوَ سَمَّىٰكُمُ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ مِن قَبْلُ وَفِى هَـٰذَا لِيَكُونَ ٱلرَّسُولُ شَهِيدًا عَلَيْكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ ۚ فَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱعْتَصِمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ هُوَ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۖ فَنِعْمَ ٱلْمَوْلَىٰ وَنِعْمَ ٱلنَّصِيرُ

This powerful ayah serves as the climax and conclusion of Surah Al-Hajj, bringing together its central themes of struggle, devotion and divine guidance. Earlier in the surah, Allah ﷻ established that humanity failed to appreciate Him as He truly deserves. This ayah now calls the believers to a different standard.

The Call to Ultimate Struggle

The command وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ فِى ٱللَّهِ (strive for Allah) uses remarkably intimate phrasing. The Quran typically says جَاهِدُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ - “strive in the path of Allah” - which suggests traveling along a path toward Him. But here, the word سَبِيلِ (path) is removed entirely. It’s simply “strive for Allah” - fi Allah, not fi sabil Allah. This deletion creates profound closeness, as if the distance of a path no longer exists between you and your Lord.

The phrase حَقَّ جِهَادِهِۦ (as He deserves to be striven for) presents us with an impossible standard. Who among us prays to Allah ﷻ as He deserves to be prayed to? Who thanks Him as He deserves to be thanked? Who remembers Him as He deserves to be remembered? If we spent every waking moment of our entire lives in worship - never sleeping, eating or drinking, only praising Allah ﷻ - would that be sufficient gratitude for His blessings? The answer is no.

Understanding jihad here requires examining the word’s deeper meaning. The root j-h-d conveys exhausting yourself, giving your absolute maximum effort against resistance. There’s a difference between shooting free throws alone and playing one-on-one basketball against an opponent. Both require effort, but facing opposition brings out your absolute best. That’s jihad - applying everything you have against internal and external resistance. This resistance manifests in many forms: your own laziness, psychological barriers, emotional struggles, family pressure, societal opposition and eventually, even military threats. But it begins within yourself, in the internal war we all face.

Divine Selection Despite Our Inadequacy

The reality is stark: we are completely underqualified to struggle for Allah ﷻ as He deserves. Consider Prophet Ibrahim (AS) building the Kaaba. When he completed this monumental act of worship that would orient billions of people in prayer for generations, he didn’t congratulate himself. Instead, he made dua: رَبَّنَا تَقَبَّلْ مِنَّا - “Our Lord, accept this from us.” He worried whether he placed each brick correctly, whether his intentions remained pure, whether he made any mistakes. He asked Allah ﷻ to accept it and to accept his tawbah. If Ibrahim (AS) felt this way, how should we feel about our efforts?

Yet immediately after this impossible standard, Allah ﷻ says هُوَ ٱجْتَبَىٰكُمْ (He has chosen you). The word اجْتَبَىٰ carries profound significance. This same word was used for Adam (AS) in the previous surah: after his mistake, فَٱجْتَبَـٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ - “then his Lord chose him.” Allah ﷻ saw good in Adam (AS) despite his error, chose him and guided him back. Now Allah ﷻ uses this identical word for all Muslims. You and I fulfill the legacy of Adam (AS) - we make mistakes, but we return to Allah ﷻ. He sees that return, that quality in us, and chooses us as Muslims.

The word ijtiba actually derives from tax collection terminology - you only collect from those who qualify, who have the requisite income. Those exempt aren’t approached. Similarly, Allah ﷻ has selected you as qualified for the mission of serving Islam and His messengers. Among billions on this planet, He sees something in you worth choosing.

None of us can say, “I don’t know why I’m Muslim. Maybe it’s just because my parents are Muslim.” No - you are Muslim because Allah ﷻ decided you possess something that will serve His cause. Every single Muslim is a contributor. Whether they realize it or not, they’re already qualified.

But here’s the serious question: is it possible to be qualified, to pass the interview, to get the job, yet not do the work? Of course. That makes the offense far graver than those who never qualified to begin with. The employer isn’t angry at rejected applicants - He’s concerned with those He hired who aren’t fulfilling their duties.

The Religion Without Difficulty

When someone protests, “I’ve been chosen? I’m not qualified! Why me? I don’t see what You see in me. This is too hard!” - Allah ﷻ responds: وَمَا جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ (He has not placed upon you in the religion any difficulty). No constriction, no tightness of the chest, no hardship whatsoever.

The particle مَا here serves to refute a misconception. Someone thinks meeting Allah’s expectations seems impossible, and Allah ﷻ corrects them: “No, you’re wrong. It’s not difficult. I’m telling you it will be easy.” This is meant to instill confidence and courage, to help you realize you can handle what lies ahead.

The Legacy of Ibrahim

Then comes a stunning statement: مِّلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ (the devoted way of your father Ibrahim). The word millah describes a nation joined together by love and devotion. You are part of that loving, devoted legacy of Ibrahim (AS).

But wait - didn’t Ibrahim (AS) face extraordinary hardships? He was thrown into fire. He confronted a tyrannical king. He smashed idols and faced the entire town’s wrath. He stood up to his own father and was expelled from his home. He took his family into the barren desert. He was commanded to sacrifice his son. The list continues. Why does Allah ﷻ mention Ibrahim (AS) immediately after saying “there’s no difficulty in this religion”?

The answer reveals Allah’s power and mercy. If Allah ﷻ could make those incredibly difficult tests easy for Ibrahim (AS) - if He could cool the fire, if He could tell the knife not to cut, if He could open every door - then what problem could possibly be too hard for you? You are the legacy of a man for whom nothing was too difficult, because Allah ﷻ was مُوَالِيهِ - the One who helped him.

You’re on the same train as the prophets. They’re in the front cars, you’re toward the back, but you’re on the same train, traveling the same tracks they traveled.

Named as Muslims

هُوَ سَمَّىٰكُمُ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ مِن قَبْلُ وَفِى هَـٰذَا (He named you Muslims before and in this). Scholars differ on whether “He” refers to Allah ﷻ or Ibrahim (AS), and both interpretations carry truth. The title “Muslim” is implied throughout the Quran, but Ibrahim (AS) specifically made dua: رَبَّنَا وَٱجْعَلْنَا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِنَآ أُمَّةً مُّسْلِمَةً لَّكَ - “Our Lord, make us Muslims submitting to You, and from our descendants a Muslim nation submitting to You.”

They’re called Muslims because when Ibrahim’s Lord told him أَسْلِمْ (submit), he responded أَسْلَمْتُ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - “I have submitted to the Lord of the worlds.” He completely surrendered, totally at peace with giving himself up to Allah ﷻ. That’s the essence of being Muslim - those who submit themselves entirely.

The Witness and the Witnessed

لِيَكُونَ ٱلرَّسُولُ شَهِيدًا عَلَيْكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ (So that the Messenger may be a witness over you, and you may be witnesses over humanity). Now that you are Muslims - the few who appreciate Allah ﷻ and struggle for Him as He deserves - the Messenger ﷺ will be made a witness against you on the Day of Judgment.

Imagine that scene. Allah ﷻ will ask the Prophet ﷺ: “Are these your followers?” He’ll confirm. “Did they do what you told them to do? Did they convey the message on your behalf, even if just a single ayah?” Whether he likes it or not, the Prophet ﷺ will have to testify truthfully about those who failed their responsibility, even if they called themselves Muslims.

Think of it as being squeezed in a witness sandwich on Judgment Day. On one side stands the Messenger ﷺ testifying: “I did my job. The responsibility was theirs.” On the other side stands the rest of humanity: “Where were these one-fifth of the world’s population? I never heard about Islam properly. My neighbor was Muslim, my colleague was Muslim, but I never saw what Islam really looks like. They never prayed in front of me. They never shared anything about the Quran. How was I supposed to know?”

This is precisely why the ayah continues: وَتَكُونُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ (so you can be witnesses against humanity). The messenger is coming after you with his testimony, so you better be able to testify about the people you were sent to. You better have delivered the message. With the same confidence the Prophet ﷺ will say “I did everything I could,” you should be able to say “I did everything I could to share Islam, to let humanity see its beauty, to demonstrate why it’s the perfect way of life.”

The sense of urgency is built right into this structure. When Muslims realize that their beloved Prophet ﷺ - the one who’s supposed to intercede on their behalf - has also been authorized to testify against them, they understand: we must take this responsibility of being witnesses to humanity seriously.

The Practical Tools

How do you accomplish this colossal task? فَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ (Establish the prayer and give the zakah). Start with the basics. The foundational acts of Islam provide your strength and grounding.

وَٱعْتَصِمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ (And hold fast to Allah). Earlier, Allah ﷻ commanded holding onto His rope - the Quran. Recite it, draw encouragement from it, memorize it, share it with others. But here the instruction is even more direct: hold onto Allah ﷻ Himself. Maintain constant contact with Him. Talk to Him in any language - He made them all and understands them all. Never lose that connection. Never forget He’s listening.

The Perfect Protector and Helper

هُوَ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ (He is your Protector). All human beings are weak and need protection, but believers receive it directly from Allah ﷻ. What better protection could possibly exist?

فَنِعْمَ ٱلْمَوْلَىٰ وَنِعْمَ ٱلنَّصِيرُ (How excellent a Protector, and how excellent a Helper!). The word mawla encompasses guardian, protective friend and patron. The word nasir describes help against enemies, aid in difficult circumstances - like soldiers calling for backup and reinforcements in critical moments of need. When you’re in deep trouble and no one else can help you, there is no better helper than Allah ﷻ.

The Surah’s Beautiful Closure

Notice how this surah concludes by answering its opening. At the beginning of Surah Al-Hajj, people on the Day of Judgment find no protection. Mothers drop their nursing children. Nobody has any guardian or helper. But at the end, a specific group of people - the believers - are told: you have the ultimate protection. فَنِعْمَ ٱلْمَوْلَىٰ - how beautiful is that resolution?

All humanity was warned that Judgment Day is coming and they’ll find no protection. But the believers are assured they couldn’t find a better Protector, a more excellent Helper. This is the gift and responsibility of being chosen by Allah ﷻ - underqualified yet selected, burdened yet unburdened, tested yet supported, witnessed yet witnessing, struggling yet promised ease, protected and aided by the Best of Protectors and the Most Excellent of Helpers.
